DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: DRUG OVERVIEW & INDICATIONS
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Drug Overview & Indications
ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION
Manufacturer: Camber Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (Piscataway, NJ) | Manufactured by: Aspiro Pharma Limited, India | Initial
U.S. Approval: 1951 | Revised: 08/2025
BOXED WARNING: RISK OF MEDICATION ERRORS AND HEPATOTOXICITY
Take care when prescribing, preparing, and administering acetaminophen injection to avoid dosing errors which could
result in accidental overdose and death. Acetaminophen has been associated with cases of acute liver failure, at times
resulting in liver transplant and death. Most cases of liver injury are associated with doses that exceed the
recommended maximum daily limits, and often involve more than one acetaminophen- containing product.
1. INDICATIONS AND USAGE
Acetaminophen injection is indicated for:
 Management of mild to moderate pain in adult and pediatric patients 2 years and older.
 Management of moderate to severe pain with adjunctive opioid analgesics in adult and pediatric patients 2 years and
older.
 Reduction of fever in adult and pediatric patients.

DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION | SUBSECTION: ADULTS AND ADOLESCENTS
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Dosage and Administration | Subsection: Adults and Adolescents
2. DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION
2.1 General Dosing Information
Acetaminophen injection may be given as a single or repeated dose for the treatment of acute pain or fever. No dose
adjustment is required when converting between oral acetaminophen and acetaminophen injection dosing in adults and
adolescents weighing 50 kg and above. Calculated maximum daily dose is based on ALL routes of administration and
ALL products containing acetaminophen. Exceeding the maximum mg/kg daily dose may result in hepatic injury,
including risk of liver failure and death.
2.2 Recommended Dosage: Adults and Adolescents
Adults and adolescents weighing 50 kg and over: 1000 mg every 6 hours OR 650 mg every 4 hours. Maximum single
dose: 1000 mg. Minimum dosing interval: 4 hours. Maximum daily dose: 4000 mg per day (all routes + all
acetaminophen- containing products).
Adults and adolescents weighing under 50 kg: 15 mg/kg every 6 hours OR 12.5 mg/kg every 4 hours. Maximum single
dose: 15 mg/kg. Minimum dosing interval: 4 hours. Maximum daily dose: 75 mg/kg per day (all routes + all
acetaminophen- containing products).

DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION | SUBSECTION: INTRAVENOUS
ADMINISTRATION INSTRUCTIONS
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Dosage and Administration | Subsection: Intravenous Administration Instructions
2.5 Instructions for Intravenous Administration
Acetaminophen injection must be administered only as a 15- minute intravenous infusion. Use aseptic technique when
preparing for IV infusion.
For adults and adolescents weighing ≥50 kg requiring 1000 mg doses: administer by inserting a vented IV set through
the septum of the 100 mL vial. May be administered without further dilution. Examine contents before administration —
DO NOT USE if particulate matter or discoloration is observed.
For doses less than 1000 mg: withdraw the appropriate dose (650 mg or weight- based) from an intact sealed container
and place in a separate empty sterile container for IV infusion. The entire 100 mL container is NOT intended for patients
weighing less than 50 kg.
For small- volume pediatric doses up to 60 mL: place in a syringe and administer over 15 minutes using a syringe pump.
Once the container seal has been penetrated or contents transferred to another container, administer within 6 hours. Do
not add other medications to the solution. Diazepam and chlorpromazine hydrochloride are physically incompatible with
acetaminophen injection — do not administer simultaneously.
3. DOSAGE FORMS AND STRENGTHS
Acetaminophen Injection: sterile, clear, colorless to slightly yellow colored, non- pyrogenic, preservative- free, isotonic
formulation for intravenous infusion. Each 100 mL glass vial contains 1000 mg acetaminophen (10 mg/mL).

DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: CONTRAINDICATIONS
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Contraindications
4. CONTRAINDICATIONS
Acetaminophen injection is contraindicated:
 In patients with known hypersensitivity to acetaminophen or to any of the excipients in the IV formulation.
 In patients with severe hepatic impairment or severe active liver disease.

DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: HEPATIC INJURY
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Hepatic Injury
5. WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS
5.1 Hepatic Injury
Administration of acetaminophen in doses higher than recommended (by ALL routes and from ALL
acetaminophen- containing products including combination products) may result in hepatic injury, including the risk of
liver failure and death.
Do NOT exceed the maximum recommended daily dose of acetaminophen. The maximum recommended daily dose
includes all routes of administration and all acetaminophen- containing products administered.
Use CAUTION when administering acetaminophen in patients with the following conditions:
 Hepatic impairment or active hepatic disease
 Alcoholism or chronic malnutrition
 Severe hypovolemia (e.g., due to dehydration or blood loss)
 Severe renal impairment (creatinine clearance ≤30 mL/min)

pages.title: Acetaminophen.pdf
pages.text:
DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: SKIN REACTIONS,
MEDICATION ERRORS & ALLERGY
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Skin Reactions, Medication Errors & Allergy
5.2 Serious Skin Reactions
Rarely, acetaminophen may cause serious skin reactions such as acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP),
Stevens- Johnson Syndrome (SJS), and toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), which can be fatal. Discontinue immediately
at the first appearance of skin rash or any sign of hypersensitivity.
5.3 Risk of Medication Errors
Take care when prescribing, preparing, and administering acetaminophen to avoid dosing errors which could result in
accidental overdose and death. Ensure that:
 The dose in milligrams (mg) and milliliters (mL) is NOT confused.
 Dosing is based on WEIGHT for patients under 50 kg.
 Infusion pumps are properly programmed.
 The total daily dose of acetaminophen from ALL sources does not exceed maximum daily limits.
5.4 Allergy and Hypersensitivity
Post- marketing reports of hypersensitivity and anaphylaxis have been reported. Clinical signs included swelling of the
face, mouth, and throat, respiratory distress, urticaria, rash, and pruritus. There were infrequent reports of
life- threatening anaphylaxis. Discontinue immediately if symptoms occur. Do NOT use in patients with acetaminophen
allergy.

Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Adverse Reactions | Subsection: Adult Population
6. ADVERSE REACTIONS
6.1 Clinical Trial Experience – Adults
A total of 1020 adult patients received acetaminophen in clinical trials. Most patients were treated with acetaminophen
1000 mg every 6 hours. The most common adverse events (incidence ≥5% and greater than placebo) were nausea,
vomiting, headache, and insomnia.

DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: ADVERSE REACTIONS | SUBSECTION: PEDIATRIC POPULATION
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Adverse Reactions | Subsection: Pediatric Population
6.1 Clinical Trial Experience – Pediatric
A total of 483 pediatric patients (72 neonates, 167 infants, 171 children, and 73 adolescents) received acetaminophen in
clinical trials. The most common adverse events (incidence ≥5%) were nausea, vomiting, constipation, and pruritus.
Additional pediatric adverse reactions (≥1% incidence): anemia, diarrhea, pyrexia, injection site pain, hypokalemia,
hypomagnesemia, hypoalbuminemia, hypophosphatemia, muscle spasm, headache, agitation, oliguria, atelectasis,
pleural effusion, pulmonary edema, stridor, wheezing, hypotension, hypertension.

pages.text:
DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: DRUG INTERACTIONS
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Drug Interactions
7. DRUG INTERACTIONS
7.1 Effects of Other Substances on Acetaminophen
Substances that induce or regulate hepatic cytochrome enzyme CYP2E1 may alter the metabolism of acetaminophen
and increase its hepatotoxic potential. Effects of ethanol are complex — excessive alcohol can induce hepatic
cytochromes, but ethanol also acts as a competitive inhibitor of the metabolism of acetaminophen.
7.2 Anticoagulants (Warfarin)
Chronic oral acetaminophen use at 4000 mg/day has been shown to cause an increase in INR (International
Normalized Ratio) in some patients stabilized on sodium warfarin as an anticoagulant. More frequent assessment of
INR may be appropriate when short- term acetaminophen is used concurrently with oral anticoagulants.

DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: PREGNANCY
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Pregnancy
8. USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS
8.1 Pregnancy
Risk Summary: Published epidemiological studies with oral acetaminophen use during pregnancy have not reported a
clear association with birth defects, miscarriage, or adverse maternal or fetal outcomes. Animal reproduction studies
have not been conducted with IV acetaminophen.
Studies in pregnant rats at doses approximately equal to the maximum human daily dose (MHDD) showed evidence of
fetotoxicity (reduced fetal weight and length) and dose- related increases in bone variations. At 1.2 times the MHDD,
areas of necrosis occurred in liver and kidney of both pregnant rats and fetuses.
Human Data: Data from 26,424 women with live born singletons exposed to oral acetaminophen during the first trimester
showed no increased risk for congenital malformations compared to unexposed controls (rate of malformations: 4.3%,
similar to the general population).

pages.text:
DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: LACTATION &
REPRODUCTIVE POTENTIAL
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Lactation & Reproductive Potential
8.2 Lactation
Limited published studies report that acetaminophen passes rapidly into human milk with similar levels in the milk and
plasma. Average neonatal doses of approximately 1% of the weight- adjusted maternal dose are reported after a single
oral administration of 1 gram. There is one well- documented report of a rash in a breast- fed infant that resolved when the
mother stopped acetaminophen use. The developmental and health benefits of breastfeeding should be considered
along with the mother's clinical need for acetaminophen.
8.3 Females and Males of Reproductive Potential
Based on animal data, use of acetaminophen may cause reduced fertility in males and females of reproductive
potential. It is not known whether these effects on fertility are reversible. Animal studies reported that oral
acetaminophen at doses 1.2 times the MHDD and greater resulted in decreased testicular weights, reduced
spermatogenesis, and reduced fertility. In female animals given the same doses, reduced implantation sites were
reported.

pages.text:
DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: PEDIATRIC USE
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Pediatric Use
8.4 Pediatric Use
Treatment of Acute Pain: The safety and effectiveness of acetaminophen for acute pain in pediatric patients ages 2 years
and older is supported by evidence from adequate and well- controlled studies in adults and safety and pharmacokinetic
data from 483 pediatric patients across all age groups.
The effectiveness for acute pain in pediatric patients younger than 2 years of age has NOT been established. In a
double- blind, placebo- controlled study of 198 patients younger than 2 years, no difference in analgesic effect was
observed.
Treatment of Fever: Safety and effectiveness for fever treatment in pediatric patients including premature neonates born
at ≥32 weeks gestational age is supported by well- controlled studies in adults, clinical studies in 244 pediatric patients ≥2
years, and safety/pharmacokinetic data from 239 patients younger than 2 years.

pages.text:
DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: GERIATRIC, HEPATIC &
RENAL IMPAIRMENT
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Geriatric, Hepatic & Renal Impairment
8.5 Geriatric Use
Of subjects in clinical studies, 15% were age 65 and over, and 5% were age 75 and over. No overall differences in safety
or effectiveness were observed between geriatric and younger subjects. Greater sensitivity of some older individuals
cannot be ruled out.
8.6 Patients with Hepatic Impairment
Acetaminophen injection is CONTRAINDICATED in patients with severe hepatic impairment or severe active liver
disease. Should be used with CAUTION in patients with hepatic impairment or active liver disease. A reduced total daily
dose may be warranted.
8.7 Patients with Renal Impairment
In cases of severe renal impairment (creatinine clearance ≤30 mL/min), LONGER dosing intervals and a REDUCED
total daily dose of acetaminophen may be warranted.

DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: OVERDOSAGE
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Overdosage
10. OVERDOSAGE
Signs and Symptoms
In acute acetaminophen overdosage, dose- dependent, potentially fatal hepatic necrosis is the most serious adverse
effect. Renal tubular necrosis, hypoglycemic coma, and thrombocytopenia may also occur.
Plasma acetaminophen levels >300 mcg/mL at 4 hours after oral ingestion were associated with hepatic damage in 90%
of patients. Minimal hepatic damage is anticipated if plasma levels at 4 hours are <150 mcg/mL or <37.5 mcg/mL at 12
hours after ingestion.
Early symptoms following a potentially hepatotoxic overdose: nausea, vomiting, diaphoresis, and general malaise.
Clinical and laboratory evidence of hepatic toxicity may not be apparent until 48 to 72 hours post- ingestion.
Treatment
If acetaminophen overdose is suspected: obtain a serum acetaminophen assay as soon as possible, but no sooner than
4 hours following oral ingestion. Obtain liver function studies initially and repeat at 24- hour intervals.
Administer the antidote N- acetylcysteine (NAC) as early as possible. The acetaminophen level can be plotted against
time since oral ingestion on the Rumack- Matthew nomogram. The lower toxic line is equivalent to 150 mcg/mL at 4 hours
and 37.5 mcg/mL at 12 hours.
If serum level is ABOVE the lower line: administer the entire course of NAC treatment. If BELOW the lower line: withhold
NAC therapy.
For additional information: call a poison control center at 1- 800- 222- 1222.

DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION: MECHANISM OF ACTION &
PHARMACOKINETICS
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Mechanism of Action & Pharmacokinetics
12. CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY
12.1 Mechanism of Action
The precise mechanism of the analgesic and antipyretic properties of acetaminophen is not established but is thought to
primarily involve central actions.
12.2 Pharmacodynamics
Acetaminophen has demonstrated analgesic and antipyretic activities in animal and human studies. Single doses up to
3000 mg and repeated doses of 1000 mg every 6 hours for 48 hours have not been shown to cause a significant effect
on platelet aggregation. Clinical studies showed no significant changes in bleeding time after multiple doses.
12.3 Pharmacokinetics
The maximum concentration (Cmax) occurs at the end of the 15- minute IV infusion. Compared to the same dose of oral
acetaminophen, the Cmax following IV administration is up to 70% higher, while overall exposure (AUC) is very similar.
At therapeutic levels, binding to plasma proteins is low (10% to 25%). Acetaminophen appears widely distributed
throughout most body tissues except fat.
Metabolism and Excretion
Acetaminophen is primarily metabolized in the liver by first- order kinetics via three principal pathways: (1) conjugation
with glucuronide, (2) conjugation with sulfate, and (3) oxidation via cytochrome P450 enzyme CYP2E1 to form the
reactive intermediate NAPQI. With therapeutic doses, NAPQI undergoes rapid conjugation with glutathione. More than
90% of the administered dose is excreted in the urine within 24 hours.

DRUG: ACETAMINOPHEN INJECTION | SECTION: CLINICAL STUDIES
Drug: Acetaminophen Injection | Section: Clinical Studies
14. CLINICAL STUDIES
14.1 Adult Acute Pain
Pain Study 1: Evaluated repeated doses of acetaminophen 1000 mg vs. placebo every 6 hours for 24 hours in 101
patients with moderate to severe pain following total hip or knee replacement. Acetaminophen was statistically superior
to placebo for reduction in pain intensity over 24 hours. There was an attendant decrease in opioid consumption.
Pain Study 2: Evaluated repeated doses of acetaminophen 1000 mg every 6 hours or 650 mg every 4 hours for 24 hours
versus placebo in 244 patients with moderate to severe postoperative pain after abdominal laparoscopic surgery.
Patients receiving acetaminophen experienced a statistically significant greater reduction in pain intensity over 24 hours
compared to placebo.
14.2 Adult Fever
Efficacy of acetaminophen 1000 mg for adult fever was evaluated in a 6- hour, single- dose, endotoxin- induced fever
study in 60 healthy adult males. A statistically significant antipyretic effect was demonstrated through 6 hours compared
to placebo.
14.3 Pediatric Acute Pain and Fever
Acetaminophen was studied in pediatric patients in three active- controlled trials and three open- label safety and
pharmacokinetic trials.

DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: DRUG OVERVIEW & INDICATIONS
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Drug Overview & Indications
PROAIR HFA (ALBUTEROL SULFATE) INHALATION AEROSOL
Distributed by: Teva Pharmaceuticals USA, Inc. (Parsippany, NJ) | Initial U.S. Approval: 1981 | Revised: 08/2021
Drug Class & Description
Albuterol sulfate is a beta2- adrenergic agonist (bronchodilator). Pro Air HFA is a pressurized metered- dose aerosol unit
with a dose counter for oral inhalation only. Each actuation delivers 108 mcg of albuterol sulfate from the actuator
mouthpiece (equivalent to 90 mcg of albuterol base). Each canister provides 200 actuations. This product does NOT
contain chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) — propellant is HFA- 134a.
1. INDICATIONS AND USAGE
1.1 Bronchospasm
Pro Air HFA Inhalation Aerosol is indicated for the treatment or prevention of bronchospasm in patients 4 years of age
and older with reversible obstructive airway disease.
1.2 Exercise- Induced Bronchospasm
Pro Air HFA Inhalation Aerosol is indicated for the prevention of exercise- induced bronchospasm in patients 4 years of
age and older.

DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Dosage and Administration
2. DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION
2.1 Bronchospasm
For treatment of acute episodes of bronchospasm or prevention of associated symptoms, the usual dosage for adults
and children 4 years and older is 2 inhalations repeated every 4 to 6 hours. More frequent administration or a larger
number of inhalations is NOT recommended. In some patients, 1 inhalation every 4 hours may be sufficient.
2.2 Exercise- Induced Bronchospasm
The usual dosage for adults and children 4 years of age or older is 2 inhalations 15 to 30 minutes before exercise.
2.3 Administration Information
Administer Pro Air HFA by oral inhalation ONLY. Shake well before each spray.
Priming: Prime the inhaler before using for the first time and in cases where the inhaler has not been used for more than
2 weeks, by releasing 3 sprays into the air, away from the face.
Cleaning: Wash the plastic mouthpiece with warm running water for 30 seconds, shake off excess water, and air dry
thoroughly at least once a week. If the patient has more than one Pro Air HFA inhaler, wash each one separately to
prevent attaching the wrong canister to the wrong actuator.
Dose Counter: Displays 200 after priming. Counts down each spray. When counter reaches 20, numbers change to RED
— contact pharmacist for refill. When counter reaches 0, background changes to solid red. Discard inhaler when counter
displays 0 or after expiration date.

DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: DOSAGE FORMS & CONTRAINDICATIONS
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Dosage Forms & Contraindications
3. DOSAGE FORMS AND STRENGTHS
Pro Air HFA is an inhalation aerosol supplied as an 8.5 g / 200 actuations pressurized aluminum canister with a red
plastic actuator with a dose counter and white dust cap, in boxes of one. Each actuation delivers 108 mcg of albuterol
sulfate from the actuator mouthpiece (equivalent to 90 mcg of albuterol base).
4. CONTRAINDICATIONS
Pro Air HFA Inhalation Aerosol is CONTRAINDICATED in patients with a history of hypersensitivity to albuterol and any
other Pro Air HFA Inhalation Aerosol components. Rare cases of hypersensitivity reactions, including urticaria,
angioedema, and rash have been reported.

pages.text:
DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: BRONCHOSPASM &
ASTHMA DETERIORATION
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Bronchospasm & Asthma Deterioration
5. WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS
5.1 Paradoxical Bronchospasm
Pro Air HFA can produce paradoxical bronchospasm that may be LIFE THREATENING. If paradoxical bronchospasm
occurs, Pro Air HFA should be DISCONTINUED IMMEDIATELY and alternative therapy instituted. Paradoxical
bronchospasm, when associated with inhaled formulations, frequently occurs with the first use of a new canister.
5.2 Deterioration of Asthma
Asthma may deteriorate acutely over hours or chronically over several days or longer. If the patient needs more doses of
Pro Air HFA than usual, this may be a marker of destabilization of asthma and requires re- evaluation of the patient and
treatment regimen, giving special consideration to the possible need for anti- inflammatory treatment, e.g.,
corticosteroids.
5.3 Use of Anti- Inflammatory Agents
The use of beta- adrenergic- agonist bronchodilators alone may not be adequate to control asthma in many patients. Early
consideration should be given to adding anti- inflammatory agents, e.g., corticosteroids, to the therapeutic regimen.

pages.text:
DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: CARDIOVASCULAR,
HYPERSENSITIVITY & OTHER
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Cardiovascular, Hypersensitivity & Other
5.4 Cardiovascular Effects
Pro Air HFA, like other beta- adrenergic agonists, can produce clinically significant cardiovascular effects as measured by
pulse rate, blood pressure, and/or symptoms. Beta- agonists have been reported to produce ECG changes, such as
flattening of the T wave, prolongation of the QTc interval, and ST segment depression. Use with caution in patients with
cardiovascular disorders, especially coronary insufficiency, cardiac arrhythmias, and hypertension.
5.5 Do Not Exceed Recommended Dose
Fatalities have been reported in association with EXCESSIVE USE of inhaled sympathomimetic drugs in patients with
asthma. The exact cause of death is unknown, but cardiac arrest following an unexpected development of a severe
acute asthmatic crisis and subsequent hypoxia is suspected.
5.7 Coexisting Conditions
Use with caution in patients with: cardiovascular disorders (coronary insufficiency, cardiac arrhythmias, hypertension),
convulsive disorders, hyperthyroidism, diabetes mellitus, and in patients unusually responsive to sympathomimetic
amines. Large doses of intravenous albuterol have been reported to aggravate preexisting diabetes mellitus and
ketoacidosis.
5.8 Hypokalemia
Pro Air HFA may produce significant hypokalemia in some patients, possibly through intracellular shunting, which has the
potential to produce adverse cardiovascular effects. The decrease is usually transient and does not require
supplementation.

6.1 Clinical Trials Experience
A total of 1090 subjects were treated with Pro Air HFA during the worldwide clinical development program. The most
common adverse reactions (≥3.0% and greater than placebo) are headache, tachycardia, pain, dizziness, pharyngitis,

6.2 Postmarketing Experience
Post- approval adverse reactions include: urticaria, angioedema, rash, bronchospasm, hoarseness, oropharyngeal

pages.text:
DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: DRUG INTERACTIONS
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Drug Interactions
7. DRUG INTERACTIONS
Other short- acting sympathomimetic aerosol bronchodilators should NOT be used concomitantly with Pro Air HFA. If
additional adrenergic drugs are to be administered by any route, they should be used with caution to avoid deleterious
cardiovascular effects.
7.1 Beta- Blockers
Beta- adrenergic receptor blocking agents not only block the pulmonary effect of beta- agonists but may produce severe
bronchospasm in asthmatic patients. Therefore, patients with asthma should NOT normally be treated with
beta- blockers. Under certain circumstances (e.g., as prophylaxis after MI), consider cardioselective beta- blockers,
administered with caution.
7.2 Diuretics
ECG changes and/or hypokalemia from non- potassium sparing diuretics (loop or thiazide) can be acutely worsened by
beta- agonists, especially when the recommended dose is exceeded. Caution is advised in coadministration. Consider
monitoring potassium levels.
7.3 Digoxin
Mean decreases of 16% and 22% in serum digoxin levels were demonstrated after single- dose IV and oral administration
of albuterol, respectively, in volunteers who had received digoxin for 10 days. Monitor serum digoxin levels in patients
receiving both albuterol and digoxin chronically.
7.4 MAO Inhibitors or Tricyclic Antidepressants
Pro Air HFA should be administered with EXTREME CAUTION to patients being treated with monoamine oxidase
(MAO) inhibitors or tricyclic antidepressants, or within 2 weeks of discontinuation of such agents, because the action of
albuterol on the cardiovascular system may be potentiated. Consider alternative therapy in patients taking MAO
inhibitors or tricyclic antidepressants.

pages.text:
DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: PREGNANCY
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Pregnancy
8. USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS
8.1 Pregnancy
Risk Summary: There are no randomized clinical studies of use of albuterol during pregnancy. Available data from
published epidemiological studies and postmarketing case reports of pregnancy outcomes following inhaled albuterol
use do NOT consistently demonstrate a risk of major birth defects or miscarriage.
Disease- Associated Risk: In women with poorly or moderately controlled asthma, there is an increased risk of
preeclampsia in the mother and prematurity, low birth weight, and small for gestational age in the neonate. Pregnant
women should be closely monitored.
Labor/Delivery: Because of the potential for beta- agonist interference with uterine contractility, use of Pro Air HFA for
bronchospasm during labor should be restricted to those patients in whom the benefits clearly outweigh the risk. Pro Air
HFA has NOT been approved for management of pre- term labor. Serious adverse reactions including pulmonary edema
have been reported during treatment of premature labor with beta2- agonists.

DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: LACTATION &
PEDIATRIC USE
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Lactation & Pediatric Use
8.2 Lactation
There are no available data on the presence of albuterol in human milk, the effects on the breastfed child, or the effects
on milk production. Plasma levels of albuterol after inhaled therapeutic doses are low in humans, and if present in breast
milk, albuterol has a low oral bioavailability. The developmental and health benefits of breastfeeding should be
considered along with the mother's clinical need for albuterol.
8.4 Pediatric Use
Safety and effectiveness in children 12 years of age and older is based on one 6- week clinical trial in 116 patients and
one single- dose crossover study in 58 patients.
Safety of Pro Air HFA in children 4 to 11 years of age is based on one 3- week clinical trial in 50 patients. Effectiveness in
children 4 to 11 years is extrapolated from trials in patients 12 years and older.
The safety and effectiveness of Pro Air HFA in pediatric patients BELOW THE AGE OF 4 YEARS have NOT been
established.

pages.text:
DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: GERIATRIC USE
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Geriatric Use
8.5 Geriatric Use
Clinical studies of Pro Air HFA did not include sufficient numbers of patients aged 65 and over to determine whether they
respond differently from younger patients. In general, dose selection for an elderly patient should be cautious, usually
starting at the low end of the dosing range, reflecting the greater frequency of decreased hepatic, renal, or cardiac
function, and of concomitant disease or other drug therapy.
All beta2- adrenergic agonists, including albuterol, are known to be substantially excreted by the kidney, and the risk of
toxic reactions may be GREATER in patients with impaired renal function. Because elderly patients are more likely to
have decreased renal function, care should be taken in dose selection, and it may be useful to monitor renal function.

pages.text:
DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: OVERDOSAGE
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Overdosage
10. OVERDOSAGE
Expected symptoms with overdosage are those of excessive beta- adrenergic stimulation and/or exaggeration of adverse
reactions, including: seizures, angina, hypertension or hypotension, tachycardia with rates up to 200 beats per minute,
arrhythmias, nervousness, headache, tremor, dry mouth, palpitation, nausea, dizziness, fatigue, malaise, and insomnia.
Hypokalemia may also occur.
Cardiac arrest and even death may be associated with ABUSE of Pro Air HFA Inhalation Aerosol.
Treatment
Treatment consists of discontinuation of Pro Air HFA together with appropriate symptomatic therapy. The judicious use of
a cardioselective beta- receptor blocker may be considered, bearing in mind that such medication can produce
bronchospasm. There is insufficient evidence to determine if dialysis is beneficial for overdosage of Pro Air HFA.

pages.title: Albuterol.pdf
pages.text:
DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION: MECHANISM OF ACTION
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Mechanism of Action
12. CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY
12.1 Mechanism of Action
Albuterol sulfate is a beta2- adrenergic agonist. The pharmacologic effects are attributable to activation of
beta2- adrenergic receptors on airway smooth muscle. Activation of beta2- adrenergic receptors leads to the activation of
adenylcyclase and an increase in intracellular cyclic AMP (cAMP).
Increased cyclic AMP is associated with activation of protein kinase A, which inhibits the phosphorylation of myosin and
lowers intracellular ionic calcium concentrations, resulting in muscle relaxation. Albuterol relaxes the smooth muscle of
ALL airways, from the trachea to the terminal bronchioles. Albuterol acts as a functional antagonist to relax the airway
irrespective of the spasmogen involved, thus protecting against all bronchoconstrictor challenges.
Increased cyclic AMP concentrations are also associated with the inhibition of release of mediators from mast cells in the
airway.

pages.title: Albuterol.pdf
pages.text:
DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION: PHARMACOKINETICS
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Pharmacokinetics
12.2 Pharmacokinetics
The systemic levels of albuterol are LOW after inhalation of recommended doses. In a crossover study in healthy
volunteers, high cumulative doses of Pro Air HFA (1,080 mcg administered over one hour) yielded mean peak plasma
concentrations (Cmax) of approximately 4,100 pg/mL. The terminal plasma half- life of albuterol delivered by Pro Air HFA
was approximately 6 hours.
In 11 healthy pediatric volunteers (4 to 11 years), a single dose of Pro Air HFA (180 mcg albuterol base) yielded a Cmax
of 1,100 pg/mL. The terminal plasma half- life was approximately 166 minutes.
Metabolism and Elimination: The primary enzyme responsible for albuterol metabolism is SULT1A3 (sulfotransferase).
The primary route of elimination is through renal excretion (80% to 100%) of either the parent compound or the primary
metabolite. Less than 20% of the drug is detected in feces.
Renal Impairment: In subjects with creatinine clearance of 7 to 53 mL/min, there was a 67% decline in albuterol
clearance compared to healthy volunteers. Renal disease had no effect on half- life. Caution should be used when
administering high doses to patients with renal impairment.

pages.title: Albuterol.pdf
pages.text:
DRUG: ALBUTEROL SULFATE (PROAIR HFA) | SECTION: CLINICAL STUDIES
Drug: Albuterol Sulfate (Pro Air HFA) | Section: Clinical Studies
14. CLINICAL STUDIES
14.1 Bronchospasm Associated with Asthma
Adults and Adolescents (≥12 years): In a 6- week, randomized, double- blind, placebo- controlled trial, Pro Air HFA (58
patients) was compared to matched placebo (58 patients) in asthmatic patients 12 to 76 years of age at a dose of 180
mcg albuterol four times daily. Two inhalations of Pro Air HFA produced significantly greater improvement in FEV1 over
the pre- treatment value than matched placebo.
In this study, 31 of 58 patients treated with Pro Air HFA achieved a 15% increase in FEV1 within 30 minutes post- dose on
Day 1. Median time to onset: 8.2 minutes. Median time to peak effect: 47 minutes. Median duration of effect:
approximately 3 hours. In some patients, duration was as long as 6 hours.
Pediatric Patients (4 to 11 years): In a 3- week, randomized, double- blind, placebo- controlled trial in 50 asthmatic
children, two inhalations of HFA albuterol produced significantly greater improvement in FEV1 than matched placebo.
Median time to onset: 10 minutes. Median time to peak effect: 31 minutes. Duration of effect: approximately 4 hours.
14.2 Exercise- Induced Bronchospasm
In a randomized, single- dose, crossover study in 24 adults and adolescents with exercise- induced bronchospasm (EIB),
two inhalations of Pro Air HFA taken 30 minutes before exercise prevented EIB for one hour following exercise in 83%
(20 of 24) of patients, compared to 25% (6 of 24) of patients who received placebo.

pages.text:
DRUG: AMLODIPINE BESYLATE (NORVASC) | SECTION: DRUG OVERVIEW & INDICATIONS
Drug: Amlodipine Besylate (Norvasc) | Section: Drug Overview & Indications
NORVASC (AMLODIPINE BESYLATE) TABLETS
Distributed by: Viatris Specialty LLC (Morgantown, WV 26505, U.S.A.) | Drug Class: Calcium Channel Blocker
(Dihydropyridine) | Initial U.S. Approval: 1992 | Revised: 02/2023
1. INDICATIONS AND USAGE
1.1 Hypertension
Norvasc is indicated for the treatment of hypertension, to lower blood pressure. Lowering blood pressure reduces the risk
of fatal and nonfatal cardiovascular events, primarily strokes and myocardial infarctions. Norvasc may be used alone or
in combination with other antihypertensive agents.
Control of high blood pressure should be part of comprehensive cardiovascular risk management, including lipid control,
diabetes management, antithrombotic therapy, smoking cessation, exercise, and limited sodium intake. Many patients
will require more than one drug to achieve blood pressure goals.
1.2 Coronary Artery Disease (CAD)
Chronic Stable Angina: Norvasc is indicated for the symptomatic treatment of chronic stable angina. May be used alone
or in combination with other antianginal agents.
Vasospastic Angina (Prinzmetal's or Variant Angina): Norvasc is indicated for the treatment of confirmed or suspected
vasospastic angina. May be used as monotherapy or in combination with other antianginal agents.
Angiographically Documented CAD: In patients with recently documented CAD by angiography and without heart failure
or an ejection fraction less than 40%, Norvasc is indicated to reduce the risk of hospitalization for angina and to reduce
the risk of a coronary revascularization procedure.

DRUG: AMLODIPINE BESYLATE (NORVASC) | SECTION: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION | SUBSECTION: ADULTS
Drug: Amlodipine Besylate (Norvasc) | Section: Dosage and Administration | Subsection: Adults
2. DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION
2.1 Adults
Hypertension: The usual initial antihypertensive oral dose of Norvasc is 5 mg once daily. The maximum dose is 10 mg
once daily. Small, fragile, or elderly patients, or patients with hepatic insufficiency may be started on 2.5 mg once daily.
This 2.5 mg dose may also be used when adding Norvasc to other antihypertensive therapy.
Adjust dosage according to blood pressure goals. In general, wait 7 to 14 days between titration steps. Titrate more
rapidly if clinically warranted, provided the patient is assessed frequently.
Angina (Chronic Stable or Vasospastic): The recommended dose is 5 to 10 mg, with the lower dose suggested in the
elderly and in patients with hepatic insufficiency. Most patients will require 10 mg for adequate effect.
Coronary Artery Disease: The recommended dose range is 5 to 10 mg once daily. In clinical studies, the majority of
patients required 10 mg.

pages.text:
DRUG: AMLODIPINE BESYLATE (NORVASC) | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS
Drug: Amlodipine Besylate (Norvasc) | Section: Warnings and Precautions
5. WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS
5.1 Hypotension
Symptomatic hypotension is possible, particularly in patients with severe aortic stenosis. Because of the gradual onset of
action of amlodipine, acute hypotension is unlikely.
5.2 Increased Angina or Myocardial Infarction
Worsening angina and acute myocardial infarction can develop after starting or increasing the dose of Norvasc,
particularly in patients with severe obstructive coronary artery disease.
5.3 Patients with Hepatic Failure
Because Norvasc is extensively metabolized by the liver and the plasma elimination half- life (t1/2) is 56 hours in patients
with impaired hepatic function, TITRATE SLOWLY when administering Norvasc to patients with severe hepatic
impairment. Start at 2.5 mg once daily.

6. ADVERSE REACTIONS
6.1 Clinical Trials Experience
Norvasc has been evaluated for safety in more than 11,000 patients in U.S. and foreign clinical trials. Treatment with
Norvasc was well- tolerated at doses up to 10 mg daily. Most adverse reactions were of mild or moderate severity.
Discontinuation of Norvasc because of adverse reactions was required in only about 1.5% of patients.
The most common adverse reaction to amlodipine is peripheral edema, which occurred in a dose- related manner.

Additional Adverse Reactions (<1% in placebo- controlled trials, >0.1%):
Cardiovascular: arrhythmia (including ventricular tachycardia and atrial fibrillation), bradycardia, chest pain, peripheral
ischemia, syncope, tachycardia, vasculitis.
CNS/Peripheral Nervous System: hypoesthesia, peripheral neuropathy, paresthesia, tremor, vertigo.
Gastrointestinal: anorexia, constipation, dysphagia, diarrhea, flatulence, pancreatitis, vomiting, gingival hyperplasia.
Skin: angioedema, erythema multiforme, pruritus, erythematous rash, maculopapular rash.
6.2 Postmarketing Experience
Gynecomastia has been reported infrequently. Jaundice and hepatic enzyme elevations (mostly consistent with
cholestasis or hepatitis), in some cases severe enough to require hospitalization, have been reported. A possible
association between extrapyramidal disorder and amlodipine has also been revealed in postmarketing reporting.
Note: Norvasc has been used safely in patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, well- compensated
congestive heart failure, coronary artery disease, peripheral vascular disease, diabetes mellitus, and abnormal lipid
profiles.

pages.text:
DRUG: AMLODIPINE BESYLATE (NORVASC) | SECTION: DRUG INTERACTIONS
Drug: Amlodipine Besylate (Norvasc) | Section: Drug Interactions
7. DRUG INTERACTIONS
7.1 Impact of Other Drugs on Amlodipine
CYP3A Inhibitors: Co- administration with CYP3A inhibitors (moderate and strong) results in increased systemic
exposure to amlodipine and may require dose reduction. Monitor for symptoms of hypotension and edema when
amlodipine is co- administered with CYP3A inhibitors. Example: Diltiazem 180 mg/day co- administered with amlodipine 5
mg in elderly hypertensive patients resulted in a 60% increase in amlodipine systemic exposure.
CYP3A Inducers: Blood pressure should be closely monitored when amlodipine is co- administered with CYP3A inducers.
Sildenafil: Monitor for hypotension when sildenafil is co- administered with amlodipine. When used in combination, each
agent independently exerted its own blood pressure lowering effect.
7.2 Impact of Amlodipine on Other Drugs
Simvastatin: Co- administration of multiple doses of amlodipine 10 mg with simvastatin 80 mg resulted in a 77% increase
in exposure to simvastatin. LIMIT the dose of simvastatin in patients on amlodipine to 20 mg daily.
Cyclosporine: A prospective study in renal transplant patients (N=11) showed an average 40% increase in trough
cyclosporine levels when concomitantly treated with amlodipine. Frequent monitoring of trough cyclosporine levels is
recommended.
Tacrolimus: A prospective study in healthy Chinese volunteers (N=9) with CYP3A5 expressers showed a 2.5 to 4- fold
increase in tacrolimus exposure when co- administered with amlodipine. Irrespective of CYP3A5 genotype status, the
possibility of an interaction cannot be excluded. Frequent monitoring of trough tacrolimus levels is recommended.
No Effect on: Amlodipine does not affect exposure to atorvastatin, digoxin, ethanol, or the warfarin prothrombin response
time.

pages.text:
DRUG: AMLODIPINE BESYLATE (NORVASC) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: PREGNANCY
Drug: Amlodipine Besylate (Norvasc) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Pregnancy
8. USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS
8.1 Pregnancy
Risk Summary: The limited available data based on post- marketing reports with Norvasc use in pregnant women are not
sufficient to inform a drug- associated risk for major birth defects and miscarriage. There are risks to the mother and fetus
associated with poorly controlled hypertension in pregnancy.
Disease- Associated Risk: Hypertension in pregnancy increases the maternal risk for pre- eclampsia, gestational diabetes,
premature delivery, and delivery complications (e.g., need for cesarean section and post- partum hemorrhage).
Hypertension increases the fetal risk for intrauterine growth restriction and intrauterine death. Pregnant women with
hypertension should be carefully monitored.
Animal Data: No evidence of teratogenicity or other embryo/fetal toxicity was found when pregnant rats and rabbits were
treated orally with amlodipine maleate at doses approximately 10 and 20 times the maximum recommended human dose
(MRHD), respectively. However, for rats, litter size was significantly decreased (by about 50%) and intrauterine deaths
were significantly increased (about 5- fold). Amlodipine has been shown to prolong both the gestation period and the
duration of labor in rats at this dose.

pages.text:
DRUG: AMLODIPINE BESYLATE (NORVASC) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: LACTATION,
PEDIATRIC & GERIATRIC USE
Drug: Amlodipine Besylate (Norvasc) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Lactation, Pediatric & Geriatric Use
8.2 Lactation
Limited available data from a published clinical lactation study reports that amlodipine is present in human milk at an
estimated median relative infant dose of 4.2%. No adverse effects of amlodipine on the breastfed infant have been
observed. There is no available information on the effects of amlodipine on milk production.
8.4 Pediatric Use
Norvasc (2.5 to 5 mg daily) is effective in lowering blood pressure in patients 6 to 17 years. Two hundred sixty- eight
hypertensive patients aged 6 to 17 years were randomized to Norvasc 2.5 or 5 mg once daily for 4 weeks. Adverse
events were similar to those seen in adults.
Effect of Norvasc on blood pressure in patients LESS THAN 6 YEARS OF AGE is NOT KNOWN.
8.5 Geriatric Use
In general, dose selection for an elderly patient should be cautious, usually starting at the low end of the dosing range
(2.5 mg once daily), reflecting the greater frequency of decreased hepatic, renal, or cardiac function, and of concomitant
disease or other drug therapy.
Elderly patients have DECREASED CLEARANCE of amlodipine with a resulting increase of AUC of approximately 40 to
60%. A lower initial dose may be required.

pages.text:
DRUG: AMLODIPINE BESYLATE (NORVASC) | SECTION: OVERDOSAGE
Drug: Amlodipine Besylate (Norvasc) | Section: Overdosage
10. OVERDOSAGE
Overdosage might be expected to cause excessive peripheral vasodilation with marked hypotension and possibly a
reflex tachycardia. In humans, experience with intentional overdosage of Norvasc is limited.
Animal Data: Single oral doses equivalent to 40 mg amlodipine/kg in mice and 100 mg/kg in rats caused deaths. Single
oral doses equivalent to 4 or more mg amlodipine/kg in dogs caused marked peripheral vasodilation and hypotension.
Treatment of Overdosage
If massive overdose occurs: initiate active cardiac and respiratory monitoring. Frequent blood pressure measurements
are essential.
If hypotension occurs: provide cardiovascular support including elevation of the extremities and judicious administration
of fluids.
If hypotension remains unresponsive to conservative measures: consider administration of vasopressors (such as
phenylephrine) with attention to circulating volume and urine output.
As Norvasc is HIGHLY PROTEIN BOUND (approximately 93%), hemodialysis is NOT likely to be of benefit in
overdosage.

DRUG: AMLODIPINE BESYLATE (NORVASC) | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION: MECHANISM OF ACTION &
PHARMACODYNAMICS
Drug: Amlodipine Besylate (Norvasc) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Mechanism of Action & Pharmacodynamics
12. CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY
12.1 Mechanism of Action
Amlodipine is a dihydropyridine calcium antagonist (calcium ion antagonist or slow- channel blocker) that inhibits the
transmembrane influx of calcium ions into vascular smooth muscle and cardiac muscle. The contractile processes of
cardiac muscle and vascular smooth muscle are dependent upon the movement of extracellular calcium ions into these
cells through specific ion channels.
Amlodipine inhibits calcium ion influx selectively, with a greater effect on vascular smooth muscle cells than on cardiac
muscle cells. Within the physiologic pH range, amlodipine is an ionized compound (p Ka=8.6), and its kinetic interaction
with the calcium channel receptor is characterized by a gradual rate of association and dissociation with the receptor
binding site, resulting in a GRADUAL ONSET OF EFFECT.
Amlodipine is a peripheral arterial vasodilator that acts directly on vascular smooth muscle to cause a reduction in
peripheral vascular resistance and reduction in blood pressure.
Mechanism in Angina:
Exertional Angina: Norvasc reduces total peripheral resistance (afterload) against which the heart works and reduces the
rate pressure product, and thus myocardial oxygen demand, at any given level of exercise.
Vasospastic Angina: Norvasc has been demonstrated to block constriction and restore blood flow in coronary arteries
and arterioles in response to calcium, potassium, epinephrine, serotonin, and thromboxane A2 analog. This inhibition of
coronary spasm is responsible for the effectiveness of Norvasc in vasospastic (Prinzmetal's or variant) angina.

pages.text:
DRUG: AMLODIPINE BESYLATE (NORVASC) | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION: PHARMACOKINETICS
Drug: Amlodipine Besylate (Norvasc) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Pharmacokinetics
12.2 Pharmacodynamics (Hemodynamics)
Following administration of therapeutic doses in patients with hypertension, Norvasc produces vasodilation resulting in
reduction of supine and standing blood pressures. These decreases in blood pressure are NOT accompanied by a
significant change in heart rate or plasma catecholamine levels with chronic dosing. With chronic once- daily oral
administration, antihypertensive effectiveness is maintained for at least 24 hours.
Electrophysiologic Effects: Norvasc does NOT change sinoatrial nodal function or atrioventricular conduction in intact
animals or humans. Norvasc therapy did not alter electrocardiographic intervals or produce higher degrees of AV blocks
in clinical trials.
12.3 Pharmacokinetics
Absorption: After oral administration of therapeutic doses, absorption produces peak plasma concentrations between 6
and 12 hours. Absolute bioavailability is estimated between 64 and 90%. Bioavailability is NOT altered by the presence
of food.
Metabolism: Amlodipine is extensively (about 90%) converted to inactive metabolites via hepatic metabolism. 10% of
parent compound and 60% of metabolites are excreted in the urine. Approximately 93% of circulating drug is bound to
plasma proteins.
Elimination: Elimination from the plasma is biphasic with a terminal elimination half- life of about 30 to 50 hours.
Steady- state plasma levels of amlodipine are reached after 7 to 8 days of consecutive daily dosing.
Renal Impairment: The pharmacokinetics of amlodipine are NOT significantly influenced by renal impairment. Patients
with renal failure may therefore receive the usual initial dose.
Hepatic Impairment / Elderly: Elderly patients and patients with hepatic insufficiency have decreased clearance of
amlodipine with a resulting increase in AUC of approximately 40 to 60%. A lower initial dose may be required. A similar
increase in AUC was observed in patients with moderate to severe heart failure.

pages.text:
DRUG: AMLODIPINE BESYLATE (NORVASC) | SECTION: CLINICAL STUDIES | SUBSECTION: HYPERTENSION
Drug: Amlodipine Besylate (Norvasc) | Section: Clinical Studies | Subsection: Hypertension
14. CLINICAL STUDIES
14.1 Effects in Hypertension – Adults
The antihypertensive efficacy of Norvasc has been demonstrated in a total of 15 double- blind, placebo- controlled,
randomized studies involving 800 patients on Norvasc and 538 on placebo. Once- daily administration produced
statistically significant placebo- corrected reductions in supine and standing blood pressures at 24 hours postdose,
averaging about 12/6 mm Hg in the standing position and 13/7 mm Hg in the supine position in patients with mild to
moderate hypertension. Tolerance was not demonstrated in patients studied for up to 1 year.
14.1 Effects in Hypertension – Pediatric
Two hundred sixty- eight hypertensive patients aged 6 to 17 years were randomized first to Norvasc 2.5 or 5 mg once
daily for 4 weeks and then randomized again to the same dose or placebo for another 4 weeks. Patients receiving 2.5 mg
or 5 mg had significantly lower systolic blood pressure than those randomized to placebo. The treatment effect was
probably less than 5 mm Hg systolic on the 5 mg dose and 3.3 mm Hg systolic on the 2.5 mg dose.

DRUG: AMLODIPINE BESYLATE (NORVASC) | SECTION: CLINICAL STUDIES | SUBSECTION: CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE
Drug: Amlodipine Besylate (Norvasc) | Section: Clinical Studies | Subsection: Coronary Artery Disease
14.2 Effects in Chronic Stable Angina
Effectiveness of 5 to 10 mg/day of Norvasc in exercise- induced angina was evaluated in 8 placebo- controlled,
double- blind clinical trials of up to 6 weeks duration involving 1038 patients (684 Norvasc, 354 placebo). Significant
increases in exercise time were seen with the 10 mg dose in 5 of 8 studies. Increases in symptom- limited exercise time
averaged 12.8% (63 sec) for Norvasc 10 mg, and 7.9% (38 sec) for Norvasc 5 mg.
14.3 Effects in Vasospastic Angina
In a double- blind, placebo- controlled clinical trial of 4 weeks duration in 50 patients, Norvasc therapy decreased angina
attacks by approximately 4/week compared with a placebo decrease of approximately 1/week (p<0.01).
14.4 Effects in Documented Coronary Artery Disease (CAMELOT Study)
CAMELOT enrolled 1318 patients with CAD recently documented by angiography, without left main coronary disease
and without heart failure or ejection fraction less than 40%. Patients were randomized to Norvasc (5 to 10 mg once daily)
or placebo in addition to standard care (including aspirin 89%, statins 83%, beta- blockers 74%). Mean follow- up was 19
months.

DRUG: AMLODIPINE BESYLATE (NORVASC) | SECTION: CLINICAL STUDIES | SUBSECTION: HEART FAILURE
Drug: Amlodipine Besylate (Norvasc) | Section: Clinical Studies | Subsection: Heart Failure
14.5 Studies in Patients with Heart Failure
Norvasc has been compared to placebo in four 8 to 12 week studies of patients with NYHA Class II/III heart failure,
involving a total of 697 patients. In these studies, there was no evidence of worsened heart failure based on measures of
exercise tolerance, NYHA classification, symptoms, or left ventricular ejection fraction.
PRAISE Study: A long- term (mean 13.8 months) placebo- controlled mortality/morbidity study of Norvasc 5 to 10 mg in
1153 patients with NYHA Classes III or IV heart failure on stable doses of diuretics, digoxin, and ACE inhibitors. Norvasc
had no effect on the primary combined endpoint of all- cause mortality and cardiac morbidity. Total combined all- cause
mortality and cardiac morbidity: 222/571 (39%) for Norvasc patients vs. 246/583 (42%) for placebo patients.
PRAISE- 2 Study: Randomized patients with NYHA Class III (80%) or IV (20%) heart failure without clinical symptoms or
objective evidence of underlying ischemic disease. Follow- up mean of 33 months. There was NO statistically significant
difference between Norvasc and placebo in the primary endpoint of all- cause mortality. With Norvasc there were more
reports of pulmonary edema.

pages.text:
DRUG: ATORVASTATIN (LIPITOR) | SECTION: INDICATIONS AND USAGE
Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Indications and Usage
1. INDICATIONS AND USAGE
LIPITOR (atorvastatin calcium) is an HMG- CoA reductase inhibitor (statin) indicated for the following:
Cardiovascular Risk Reduction:
 To reduce the risk of MI, stroke, revascularization procedures, and angina in adults with multiple risk factors for CHD
but without clinically evident CHD.
 To reduce the risk of MI and stroke in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus with multiple risk factors for CHD but without
clinically evident CHD.
 To reduce the risk of non- fatal MI, fatal and non- fatal stroke, revascularization procedures, hospitalization for
congestive heart failure, and angina in adults with clinically evident CHD.
Lipid- Lowering Therapy:
 As an adjunct to diet to reduce LDL- C in adults with primary hyperlipidemia.
 As an adjunct to diet to reduce LDL- C in adults and pediatric patients aged 10 years and older with heterozygous
familial hypercholesterolemia (HeFH).
 As an adjunct to other LDL- C- lowering therapies, or alone if such treatments are unavailable, to reduce LDL- C in adults
and pediatric patients aged 10 years and older with homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HoFH).
 As an adjunct to diet for the treatment of adults with primary dysbetalipoproteinemia.
 As an adjunct to diet for the treatment of adults with hypertriglyceridemia.
Dosage Forms and Strengths: Film- coated tablets: 10 mg, 20 mg, 40 mg, and 80 mg of atorvastatin, for oral use.

pages.text:
DRUG: ATORVASTATIN (LIPITOR) | SECTION: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION | SUBSECTION: ADULT DOSING AND GENERAL
INSTRUCTIONS
Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Dosage and Administration | Subsection: Adult Dosing and General Instructions
2. DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION
2.1 Important Dosage Information
 Take LIPITOR orally once daily at any time of the day, with or without food.
 Assess LDL- C when clinically appropriate, as early as 4 weeks after initiating LIPITOR, and adjust the dosage if
necessary.
 If a dose is missed, advise patients not to take the missed dose and resume with the next scheduled dose.
2.2 Recommended Dosage in Adult Patients
The recommended starting dosage of LIPITOR is 10 mg to 20 mg once daily. The dosage range is 10 mg to 80 mg once
daily. Patients who require a reduction in LDL- C greater than 45% may be started at 40 mg once daily.
2.3 Recommended Dosage in Pediatric Patients (≥10 Years) with HeFH
The recommended starting dosage is 10 mg once daily. The dosage range is 10 mg to 20 mg once daily.
2.4 Recommended Dosage in Pediatric Patients (≥10 Years) with HoFH
The recommended starting dosage is 10 mg to 20 mg once daily. The dosage range is 10 mg to 80 mg once daily.

Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Contraindications
4. CONTRAINDICATIONS
LIPITOR is contraindicated in patients with:
 Acute liver failure or decompensated cirrhosis [see Warnings and Precautions (5.3)].
 Hypersensitivity to atorvastatin or any excipients in LIPITOR. Hypersensitivity reactions, including anaphylaxis,
angioneurotic edema, erythema multiforme, Stevens- Johnson syndrome, and toxic epidermal necrolysis, have been
reported.

pages.text:
DRUG: ATORVASTATIN (LIPITOR) | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: MYOPATHY AND
RHABDOMYOLYSIS
Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Myopathy and Rhabdomyolysis
5.1 Myopathy and Rhabdomyolysis
LIPITOR may cause myopathy (muscle pain, tenderness, or weakness with elevated creatine kinase [CK]) and
rhabdomyolysis. Acute kidney injury secondary to myoglobinuria and rare fatalities have occurred as a result of
rhabdomyolysis in patients treated with statins, including LIPITOR.
Risk Factors for Myopathy:
Age ≥65 years, uncontrolled hypothyroidism, renal impairment, concomitant use with certain drugs (including other
lipid- lowering therapies), and higher LIPITOR dosage.
Prevention and Management:
 LIPITOR exposure may be significantly increased by inhibition of CYP3A4 and/or transporters (BCRP, OATP1B1/1B3,
P- gp), increasing the risk of myopathy.
 Concomitant use of cyclosporine, gemfibrozil, tipranavir plus ritonavir, or glecaprevir plus pibrentasvir with
LIPITOR is NOT recommended.
 Cases of myopathy/rhabdomyolysis have been reported with atorvastatin co- administered with lipid- modifying doses
(>1 g/day) of niacin, fibrates, colchicine, and ledipasvir plus sofosbuvir.
 Concomitant intake of more than 1.2 liters of grapefruit juice daily is not recommended.
 Discontinue LIPITOR if markedly elevated CK levels occur or if myopathy is diagnosed or suspected.
 Temporarily discontinue LIPITOR in patients experiencing an acute or serious condition at high risk of developing
renal failure secondary to rhabdomyolysis (e.g., sepsis, shock, severe hypovolemia, major surgery, trauma, severe
metabolic or electrolyte disorders, uncontrolled epilepsy).
 Inform patients of the risk of myopathy when starting or increasing dosage. Instruct patients to promptly report
unexplained muscle pain, tenderness, or weakness, particularly if accompanied by malaise or fever.

Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: IMNM, Hepatic Dysfunction, HbA1c, Hemorrhagic Stroke
5.2 Immune- Mediated Necrotizing Myopathy (IMNM)
Rare reports of IMNM, an autoimmune myopathy, have been associated with statin use. IMNM is characterized by:
proximal muscle weakness, persistently elevated serum CK despite statin discontinuation, positive anti- HMG CoA
reductase antibody, muscle biopsy showing necrotizing myopathy, and improvement with immunosuppressive agents.
Recurrence has been reported when the same or a different statin was administered. Discontinue LIPITOR if IMNM is
suspected.
5.3 Hepatic Dysfunction
Increases in serum transaminases have been reported. Persistent increases to >3× ULN occurred in approximately 0.7%
of patients in clinical trials. Rare postmarketing reports of fatal and non- fatal hepatic failure have occurred. Patients who
consume substantial quantities of alcohol and/or have a history of liver disease may be at increased risk.
Consider liver enzyme testing before initiation and when clinically indicated. Promptly discontinue LIPITOR if serious
hepatic injury with clinical symptoms and/or hyperbilirubinemia or jaundice occurs.
5.4 Increases in HbA1c and Fasting Serum Glucose Levels
Increases in HbA1c and fasting serum glucose levels have been reported with statins, including LIPITOR. Optimize
lifestyle measures, including regular exercise, maintaining healthy body weight, and making healthy food choices.
5.5 Increased Risk of Hemorrhagic Stroke — LIPITOR 80 mg
In a post- hoc analysis of the SPARCL trial (n=2,365 adults, without CHD, with stroke or TIA within preceding 6 months),
a higher incidence of hemorrhagic stroke was seen with LIPITOR 80 mg vs. placebo (2.3% vs. 1.4%; HR: 1.68, 95% CI:
1.09–2.59; p=0.0168). Non- fatal hemorrhagic stroke was significantly higher in the LIPITOR group (1.6% vs. 0.7%).
Consider the risk/benefit of LIPITOR 80 mg in patients with recent hemorrhagic stroke.

DRUG: ATORVASTATIN (LIPITOR) | SECTION: ADVERSE REACTIONS | SUBSECTION: LIVER ENZYMES, KEY STUDIES, AND
POSTMARKETING
Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Adverse Reactions | Subsection: Liver Enzymes, Key Studies, and Postmarketing
Elevations in Liver Enzyme Tests
Persistent elevations in serum transaminases (>3× ULN on 2+ occasions) occurred in 0.7% of LIPITOR- treated patients
in clinical trials. The incidence by dose was: 0.2% (10 mg), 0.2% (20 mg), 0.6% (40 mg), and 2.3% (80 mg).
Treating to New Targets Study (TNT)
In TNT (n=10,001 patients with clinically evident CHD; LIPITOR 10 mg vs. 80 mg), the high- dose group had more serious
adverse reactions (1.8% vs. 1.4%) and discontinuations due to adverse reactions (9.9% vs. 8.1%) over 4.9 years.
Persistent transaminase elevations (≥3× ULN) occurred in 1.3% (80 mg) vs. 0.2% (10 mg). Elevations of CK (≥10× ULN)
were 0.3% vs. 0.1%.
SPARCL Study (Stroke/TIA Population)
In SPARCL (n=4,731; LIPITOR 80 mg vs. placebo; 4.9 years), persistent hepatic transaminase elevations occurred in
0.9% (LIPITOR) vs. 0.1% (placebo). Diabetes was reported in 6.1% (LIPITOR) vs. 3.8% (placebo). LIPITOR 80 mg
reduced ischemic stroke (9.2% vs. 11.6%) but increased hemorrhagic stroke (2.3% vs. 1.4%).
Pediatric Experience (HeFH, Ages 10–17)
In a 26- week controlled study (n=140 LIPITOR, n=47 placebo), the safety and tolerability profile of LIPITOR 10 to 20 mg
daily in pediatric patients with HeFH was generally similar to placebo.

Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Drug Interactions | Subsection: Decreased Exposure and LIPITOR Effects on Other Drugs
7.2 Drug Interactions that may Decrease Exposure to LIPITOR
Rifampin:
Concomitant administration of LIPITOR with rifampin (a CYP3A4 inducer and OATP1B1 inhibitor) can lead to variable
reductions in atorvastatin plasma concentrations. Due to the dual interaction mechanism of rifampin, delayed
administration of LIPITOR after rifampin has been associated with significant reductions in atorvastatin concentrations.
Intervention: Administer LIPITOR and rifampin simultaneously.

pages.text:
DRUG: ATORVASTATIN (LIPITOR) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: PREGNANCY
Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Pregnancy
8.1 Pregnancy
Risk Summary: Discontinue LIPITOR when pregnancy is recognized. Alternatively, consider the ongoing therapeutic
needs of the individual patient. LIPITOR decreases synthesis of cholesterol and possibly other biologically active
substances derived from cholesterol; therefore, LIPITOR may cause fetal harm when administered to pregnant patients
based on its mechanism of action. Treatment of hyperlipidemia is not generally necessary during pregnancy, as
atherosclerosis is a chronic process.
Available data from case series and prospective and retrospective observational cohort studies over decades of use with
statins in pregnant women have not identified a drug- associated risk of major congenital malformations. Published data
with LIPITOR specifically are insufficient to determine risk of miscarriage.
Human Data:
A Medicaid cohort linkage study of 1,152 statin- exposed pregnant women vs. 886,996 controls did not find a significant
teratogenic effect from maternal statin use in the first trimester (relative risk of congenital malformations: 1.07; 95% CI:
0.85–1.37), after adjusting for confounders including maternal age, diabetes, hypertension, obesity, and alcohol/tobacco
use.
Animal Data:
Atorvastatin was not teratogenic in rats at doses up to 300 mg/kg/day (~30× human exposure at MRHD of 80 mg based
on body surface area) or in rabbits at doses up to 100 mg/kg/day (~20× human exposure). At maternally toxic doses,
increased post- implantation loss and decreased fetal body weight were observed.
In a rat lactation study (20, 100, or 225 mg/kg/day from gestation day 7 through lactation day 20), decreased postnatal
survival and pup body weight were observed at 100–225 mg/kg/day, with development delays (rotorod performance,
acoustic startle, pinnae detachment, eye- opening). Atorvastatin crosses the rat placenta and reaches fetal liver levels
equivalent to maternal plasma.

DRUG: ATORVASTATIN (LIPITOR) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: LACTATION, PEDIATRIC USE,
AND GERIATRIC USE
Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Lactation, Pediatric Use, and Geriatric Use
8.2 Lactation
There is no information about the presence of atorvastatin in human milk or the effects on the breastfed infant. However,
another statin in this class passes into human milk. Atorvastatin and/or its metabolites are present in rat breast milk at a
milk:plasma ratio of 2:1. Statins, including LIPITOR, decrease cholesterol synthesis and may cause harm to the
breastfed infant based on mechanism of action.
Advise patients that breastfeeding is not recommended during treatment with LIPITOR.
8.4 Pediatric Use
Safety and effectiveness of LIPITOR as an adjunct to diet to reduce LDL- C have been established in pediatric patients
≥10 years with HeFH, based on a double- blind, placebo- controlled trial in 187 pediatric patients. In this limited controlled
trial, there was no significant effect on growth, sexual maturation, or menstrual cycle length.
Safety and effectiveness to reduce LDL- C have been established in pediatric patients ≥10 years with HoFH, based on an
uncontrolled trial in 8 pediatric patients.
Safety and effectiveness have NOT been established in pediatric patients younger than 10 years with HeFH or HoFH, or
in pediatric patients with other types of hyperlipidemia.
8.5 Geriatric Use
Of the total LIPITOR- treated patients in clinical trials, 15,813 (40%) were ≥65 years old and 2,800 (7%) were ≥75 years
old. No overall differences in safety or effectiveness were observed between elderly and younger patients.
Advanced age (≥65 years) is a risk factor for myopathy and rhabdomyolysis. Dose selection for elderly patients should
be cautious, recognizing greater frequency of decreased hepatic, renal, or cardiac function, concomitant disease, or
other drug therapy. Monitor geriatric patients for increased risk of myopathy.

DRUG: ATORVASTATIN (LIPITOR) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: RENAL IMPAIRMENT, HEPATIC
IMPAIRMENT, AND OVERDOSAGE
Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Renal Impairment, Hepatic Impairment, and Overdosage
8.6 Renal Impairment
Renal impairment is a risk factor for myopathy and rhabdomyolysis. Monitor all patients with renal impairment for
development of myopathy. Renal impairment does not affect the plasma concentrations of LIPITOR; therefore, no
dosage adjustment is required in patients with renal impairment.
Renal disease has no influence on the plasma concentrations or LDL- C reduction of LIPITOR. Hemodialysis is not
expected to significantly enhance LIPITOR clearance since the drug is extensively bound to plasma proteins.
8.7 Hepatic Impairment
Hepatic Status
LIPITOR Plasma Concentration Change
Chronic alcoholic liver disease / Childs- Pugh A
Cmax 4- fold higher; AUC 4- fold higher
Childs- Pugh B
Cmax ~16- fold higher; AUC ~11- fold higher
Acute liver failure or decompensated cirrhosis
CONTRAINDICATED
10. OVERDOSAGE
No specific antidotes for LIPITOR are known. There is no specific treatment for LIPITOR overdosage. In the event of an
overdose:
 Contact Poison Control Center at 1- 800- 222- 1222 for the latest recommendations.
 Due to extensive drug binding to plasma proteins, hemodialysis is NOT expected to significantly enhance LIPITOR
clearance.

pages.text:
DRUG: ATORVASTATIN (LIPITOR) | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION: MECHANISM OF ACTION,
PHARMACODYNAMICS, AND PHARMACOKINETICS
Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Mechanism of Action, Pharmacodynamics, and Pharmacokinetics
12.1 Mechanism of Action
LIPITOR is a selective, competitive inhibitor of HMG- CoA reductase, the rate- limiting enzyme that converts
3- hydroxy- 3- methylglutaryl- coenzyme A to mevalonate, a precursor of sterols including cholesterol. In animal models,
LIPITOR lowers plasma cholesterol and lipoprotein levels by: (1) inhibiting HMG- CoA reductase and cholesterol
synthesis in the liver, (2) increasing the number of hepatic LDL receptors on the cell surface to enhance uptake and
catabolism of LDL, and (3) reducing LDL production and the number of LDL particles.
12.2 Pharmacodynamics
LIPITOR and some of its metabolites are pharmacologically active in humans. The liver is the primary site of action and
the principal site of cholesterol synthesis and LDL clearance. Drug dosage, rather than systemic drug concentration,
correlates better with LDL- C reduction. Individualization of drug dosage should be based on therapeutic response.
12.3 Pharmacokinetics
Absorption:
LIPITOR is rapidly absorbed after oral administration; maximum plasma concentrations occur within 1 to 2 hours. Extent
of absorption increases proportionally with dose. The absolute bioavailability of atorvastatin (parent drug) is
approximately 14%; the systemic availability of HMG- CoA reductase inhibitory activity is approximately 30%. The low
bioavailability is attributed to presystemic clearance in gastrointestinal mucosa and/or hepatic first- pass metabolism.
Food decreases the rate and extent of absorption by approximately 25% (Cmax) and 9% (AUC), but LDL- C reduction is
similar with or without food. Evening dosing results in approximately 30% lower Cmax and AUC vs. morning dosing, but
LDL- C reduction is equivalent regardless of time of day.
Distribution:
Mean volume of distribution is approximately 381 liters. LIPITOR is ≥98% bound to plasma proteins. A blood/plasma ratio
of approximately 0.25 indicates poor drug penetration into red blood cells.
Metabolism:
LIPITOR is extensively metabolized to ortho- and parahydroxylated derivatives and various beta- oxidation products. In
vitro inhibition of HMG- CoA reductase by these metabolites is equivalent to that of LIPITOR. Approximately 70% of
circulating inhibitory activity is attributed to active metabolites. Metabolism occurs primarily via CYP3A4.
Excretion:
LIPITOR and its metabolites are eliminated primarily in bile following hepatic and/or extra- hepatic metabolism; however,
the drug does not appear to undergo enterohepatic recirculation. Mean plasma elimination half- life is approximately 14
hours, but the half- life of inhibitory activity for HMG- CoA reductase is 20 to 30 hours due to active metabolites. Less than
2% of a dose is recovered in urine.

DRUG: ATORVASTATIN (LIPITOR) | SECTION: NONCLINICAL TOXICOLOGY | SUBSECTION: CARCINOGENESIS, MUTAGENESIS,
AND IMPAIRMENT OF FERTILITY
Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Nonclinical Toxicology | Subsection: Carcinogenesis, Mutagenesis, and Impairment of Fertility
13.1 Carcinogenesis, Mutagenesis, Impairment of Fertility
Carcinogenesis:
In a 2- year carcinogenicity study in rats at 10, 30, and 100 mg/kg/day, two rare tumors were found in muscle in high- dose
females (1 rhabdomyosarcoma, 1 fibrosarcoma), at plasma AUC approximately 16× the mean human exposure after an
80 mg oral dose.
In a 2- year study in mice at 100, 200, or 400 mg/kg/day, a significant increase in liver adenomas in high- dose males and
liver carcinomas in high- dose females occurred at plasma AUC approximately 6× the mean human exposure at 80 mg.
Mutagenesis:
In vitro, atorvastatin was not mutagenic or clastogenic in the Ames test (S. typhimurium and E. coli), the HGPRT forward
mutation assay in Chinese hamster lung cells, and the chromosomal aberration assay in Chinese hamster lung cells.
Atorvastatin was negative in the in vivo mouse micronucleus test.
Impairment of Fertility:
In female rats, atorvastatin at doses up to 225 mg/kg/day (56× human exposure) did not cause adverse effects on
fertility. In male rats at doses up to 175 mg/kg/day (15× human exposure), no changes in fertility were found. However, at
100 mg/kg/day for 3 months (16× human AUC at 80 mg), aplasia and aspermia in the epididymis were found in 2/10 rats;
testis and epididymal weights were lower. Male rats given 100 mg/kg/day for 11 weeks before mating showed decreased
sperm motility, spermatid head concentration, and increased abnormal sperm. Atorvastatin caused no adverse effects on
semen parameters or reproductive organ histopathology in dogs given up to 120 mg/kg/day for 2 years.

DRUG: ATORVASTATIN (LIPITOR) | SECTION: CLINICAL STUDIES | SUBSECTION: CARDIOVASCULAR RISK REDUCTION: ASCOT
AND CARDS
Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Clinical Studies | Subsection: Cardiovascular Risk Reduction: ASCOT and CARDS
14. CLINICAL STUDIES — Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease
ASCOT- LLA (Anglo- Scandinavian Cardiac Outcomes Trial):
10,305 hypertensive patients (ages 40–80, mean 63 years; 19% female; 95% White; TC ≤251 mg/dL) with ≥3 additional
cardiovascular risk factors but no prior MI were randomized to LIPITOR 10 mg/day (n=5,168) or placebo (n=5,137) and
followed for a median of 3.3 years.
LIPITOR significantly reduced the rate of coronary events (relative risk reduction 36%; LIPITOR 1.9% vs. placebo 3.0%;
p=0.0005). Revascularization procedures were reduced by 42% (1.4% vs. 2.5%). A favorable trend was seen for
fatal/non- fatal strokes (26% relative risk reduction; 1.7% vs. 2.3%; p=0.01 not pre- specified threshold). The risk reduction
was consistent regardless of age, smoking status, obesity, or renal dysfunction.
CARDS (Collaborative Atorvastatin Diabetes Study):
2,838 subjects (ages 40–75; 94% White; 68% male) with type 2 diabetes, LDL ≤160 mg/dL, TG ≤600 mg/dL, no prior
CVD history, and ≥1 additional risk factor (smoking, hypertension, retinopathy, albuminuria) were randomized to
LIPITOR 10 mg/day (n=1,429) or placebo (n=1,411) and followed for a median of 3.9 years.
Baseline characteristics: mean age 62 years, mean HbA1c 7.7%, median LDL- C 120 mg/dL, median TC 207 mg/dL.
LIPITOR significantly reduced the rate of major cardiovascular events (83 vs. 127 events; relative risk reduction 37%; HR
0.63, 95% CI 0.48–0.83; p=0.001). LIPITOR also significantly reduced: stroke risk by 48% (HR 0.52; p=0.016) and MI
risk by 42% (HR 0.58; p=0.007). The effect was consistent regardless of age, sex, or baseline lipid levels. Total deaths:
61 (LIPITOR) vs. 82 (placebo); HR 0.73, p=0.059.

DRUG: ATORVASTATIN (LIPITOR) | SECTION: CLINICAL STUDIES | SUBSECTION: HOFH IN ADULTS/PEDIATRIC AND HEFH IN
PEDIATRIC PATIENTS
Drug: Atorvastatin (Lipitor) | Section: Clinical Studies | Subsection: HoFH in Adults/Pediatric and HeFH in Pediatric Patients
HoFH — Adults and Pediatric Patients
In an uncontrolled trial, 29 patients (mean age 22 years, median 24 years; 31% <18 years) with HoFH received 20 to 80
mg/day of LIPITOR. Mean LDL- C reduction was 18%. Among 25 patients with a reduction in LDL- C, mean response was
20% (range 7%–53%; median 24%). Four patients had 7%–24% increases in LDL- C. Five patients had absent
LDL- receptor function; 2 of these also had a portacaval shunt with no significant LDL- C reduction. The remaining 3
receptor- negative patients had mean LDL- C reduction of 22%.
HeFH — Pediatric Patients (Ages 10–17, Table 12)
187 males and post- menarchal females (mean age 14.1 years; 31% female; 92% White) with HeFH or severe
hypercholesterolemia were randomized to LIPITOR (n=140) or placebo (n=47) for 26 weeks. Inclusion required LDL- C
≥190 mg/dL or LDL- C ≥160 mg/dL with positive family history of FH or documented premature cardiovascular disease.
Starting dose was 10 mg; uptitrated to 20 mg if LDL- C >130 mg/dL at 4 weeks (56% required uptitration). Mean baseline
LDL- C: 219 mg/dL (LIPITOR) vs. 230 mg/dL (placebo).

DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: BOXED WARNING AND INDICATIONS AND USAGE
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Boxed Warning and Indications and Usage
BOXED WARNING: NOT FOR TREATMENT OF OBESITY OR WEIGHT LOSS
Thyroid hormones, including SYNTHROID, either alone or with other therapeutic agents, should NOT be used for the
treatment of obesity or for weight loss.
In euthyroid patients, doses within the range of daily hormonal requirements are ineffective for weight reduction. Larger
doses may produce serious or even life- threatening manifestations of toxicity, particularly when given in association with
sympathomimetic amines such as those used for their anorectic effects.
1. INDICATIONS AND USAGE
Hypothyroidism
SYNTHROID is indicated in adult and pediatric patients, including neonates, as replacement therapy in primary
(thyroidal), secondary (pituitary), and tertiary (hypothalamic) congenital or acquired hypothyroidism.
Pituitary TSH Suppression
SYNTHROID is indicated in adult and pediatric patients, including neonates, as an adjunct to surgery and radioiodine
therapy in the management of thyrotropin- dependent well- differentiated thyroid cancer.
Limitations of Use
 SYNTHROID is NOT indicated for suppression of benign thyroid nodules and nontoxic diffuse goiter in iodine- sufficient
patients — there are no clinical benefits and overtreatment may induce hyperthyroidism.
 SYNTHROID is NOT indicated for treatment of hypothyroidism during the recovery phase of subacute thyroiditis.
