pages.text:
DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION | SUBSECTION: IMPORTANT
ADMINISTRATION INSTRUCTIONS AND DOSING CONSIDERATIONS
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Dosage and Administration | Subsection: Important Administration Instructions and Dosing
Considerations
2.1 Important Administration Instructions
Administer SYNTHROID as a single daily dose, on an empty stomach, one- half to one hour before breakfast.
Administer SYNTHROID at least 4 hours before or after drugs known to interfere with SYNTHROID absorption.
Evaluate the need for dosage adjustments when regularly administering within one hour of certain foods that may affect
SYNTHROID absorption.
For pediatric patients who cannot swallow intact tablets: crush the tablet, suspend the freshly crushed tablet in a small
amount (5–10 mL) of water, and immediately administer by spoon or dropper. Do not store the suspension. Do not
administer in soybean- based infant formula or other foods that decrease absorption.
2.2 Important Considerations for Dosing
Dosage depends on patient age, body weight, cardiovascular status, concomitant medical conditions (including
pregnancy), concomitant medications, co- administered food, and the specific nature of the condition being treated.
Dosing must be individualized.
For adult patients with primary hypothyroidism, titrate until clinically euthyroid and serum TSH returns to normal.
For secondary or tertiary hypothyroidism, serum TSH is NOT a reliable measure of dosage adequacy. Use the serum
free- T4 level to titrate until clinically euthyroid and free- T4 is in the upper half of the normal range.
Biotin interference: Advise patients to stop biotin or biotin- containing supplements at least 2 days before assessing TSH
and/or T4 levels, as biotin can produce erroneous thyroid hormone test results.
The peak therapeutic effect of a given dose of SYNTHROID may not be attained for 4 to 6 weeks.

DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION | SUBSECTION: MONITORING TSH
AND/OR THYROXINE (T4) LEVELS
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Dosage and Administration | Subsection: Monitoring TSH and/or Thyroxine (T4) Levels
2.4 Monitoring TSH and/or Thyroxine (T4) Levels
Assess the adequacy of therapy by periodic assessment of laboratory tests and clinical evaluation.
Biotin supplementation may interfere with immunoassays for TSH, T4, and T3, resulting in erroneous thyroid hormone
test results. Stop biotin and biotin- containing supplements for at least 2 days before assessing TSH and/or T4 levels.
Persistent clinical and laboratory evidence of hypothyroidism despite an apparent adequate replacement dose may
indicate inadequate absorption, poor compliance, drug interactions, or a combination of these factors.
Adults
In adult patients with primary hypothyroidism, monitor serum TSH levels after an interval of 6 to 8 weeks after any
change in dosage. On a stable appropriate replacement dosage, evaluate clinical and biochemical response every 6 to
12 months and whenever there is a change in clinical status.
Pediatric Patients
Assess the adequacy of replacement therapy by measuring both serum TSH and total or free- T4. Monitor: 2 and 4 weeks
after initiation; 2 weeks after any change in dosage; then every 3 to 12 months until growth is complete. Perform routine
clinical examination including assessment of development, mental and physical growth, and bone maturation at regular
intervals.
Failure of serum T4 to increase into the upper half of the normal range within 2 weeks of initiation and/or of serum TSH to
decrease below 20 mIU/L within 4 weeks may indicate inadequate therapy. Assess compliance, dose administered, and
method of administration before increasing the dose.
Secondary and Tertiary Hypothyroidism
Monitor serum free- T4 levels and maintain in the upper half of the normal range.

DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: CONTRAINDICATIONS AND WARNINGS | SUBSECTION:
CONTRAINDICATIONS AND SERIOUS RISKS OF OVERTREATMENT/UNDERTREATMENT
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Contraindications and Warnings | Subsection: Contraindications and Serious Risks of
Overtreatment/Undertreatment
4. CONTRAINDICATIONS
SYNTHROID is contraindicated in patients with uncorrected adrenal insufficiency.
5. WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS
5.1 Serious Risks Related to Overtreatment or Undertreatment with SYNTHROID
SYNTHROID has a narrow therapeutic index. Overtreatment or undertreatment may have negative effects on growth
and development, cardiovascular function, bone metabolism, reproductive function, cognitive function, gastrointestinal
function, and glucose and lipid metabolism in adult or pediatric patients.
In pediatric patients with congenital and acquired hypothyroidism:
 Undertreatment may adversely affect cognitive development and linear growth.
 Overtreatment is associated with craniosynostosis and acceleration of bone age.
Titrate the dose carefully and monitor response to titration to avoid these effects. Consider the potential for food or drug
interactions and adjust the administration or dosage of SYNTHROID as needed.

pages.text:
DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: CARDIAC RISKS,
MYXEDEMA COMA, ADRENAL CRISIS
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Cardiac Risks, Myxedema Coma, Adrenal Crisis
5.2 Cardiac Adverse Reactions in the Elderly and Patients with Cardiovascular
Disease
Over- treatment with levothyroxine may cause an increase in heart rate, cardiac wall thickness, and cardiac contractility,
and may precipitate angina or arrhythmias — particularly in patients with cardiovascular disease and in elderly patients.
Initiate SYNTHROID in this population at lower doses than those recommended in younger individuals or in patients
without cardiac disease. Monitor for cardiac arrhythmias during surgical procedures in patients with coronary artery
disease receiving suppressive SYNTHROID therapy. Monitor patients receiving concomitant SYNTHROID and
sympathomimetic agents for signs and symptoms of coronary insufficiency.
If cardiac symptoms develop or worsen, reduce the SYNTHROID dose or withhold for one week and restart at a lower
dose.
5.3 Myxedema Coma
Myxedema coma is a life- threatening emergency characterized by poor circulation and hypometabolism, and may result
in unpredictable absorption of levothyroxine sodium from the gastrointestinal tract. Oral thyroid hormone drug products
are NOT recommended to treat myxedema coma. Administer thyroid hormone products formulated for intravenous
administration.
5.4 Acute Adrenal Crisis in Patients with Concomitant Adrenal Insufficiency
Thyroid hormone increases metabolic clearance of glucocorticoids. Initiation of thyroid hormone therapy prior to
initiating glucocorticoid therapy may precipitate an acute adrenal crisis. Treat patients with adrenal insufficiency with
replacement glucocorticoids prior to initiating treatment with SYNTHROID.

DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: DIABETIC
CONTROL AND BONE MINERAL DENSITY
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Diabetic Control and Bone Mineral Density
5.5 Worsening of Diabetic Control
Addition of levothyroxine therapy in patients with diabetes mellitus may worsen glycemic control and result in increased
antidiabetic agent or insulin requirements. Carefully monitor glycemic control after starting, changing, or discontinuing
SYNTHROID.
5.6 Decreased Bone Mineral Density Associated with Thyroid Hormone
Over- Replacement
Increased bone resorption and decreased bone mineral density may occur as a result of levothyroxine
over- replacement, particularly in post- menopausal women.
The increased bone resorption may be associated with:
 Increased serum levels and urinary excretion of calcium and phosphorous
 Elevations in bone alkaline phosphatase
 Suppressed serum parathyroid hormone levels
Administer the minimum dose of SYNTHROID that achieves the desired clinical and biochemical response to mitigate
this risk.

DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: ADVERSE REACTIONS
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Adverse Reactions
6. ADVERSE REACTIONS
Adverse reactions associated with SYNTHROID therapy are primarily those of hyperthyroidism due to therapeutic
overdosage. They include:
System / Category
Adverse Reactions
General
Fatigue, increased appetite, weight loss, heat intolerance, fever, excessive sweating
Central Nervous System
Headache, hyperactivity, nervousness, anxiety, irritability, emotional lability, insomnia
Musculoskeletal
Tremors, muscle weakness, muscle spasm
Cardiovascular
Palpitations, tachycardia, arrhythmias, increased pulse and blood pressure, heart
failure, angina, myocardial infarction, cardiac arrest
Respiratory
Dyspnea
Gastrointestinal
Diarrhea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, elevations in liver function tests
Dermatologic
Hair loss, flushing, rash
Endocrine
Decreased bone mineral density
Reproductive
Menstrual irregularities, impaired fertility
Neurological
Seizures (reported rarely with institution of levothyroxine therapy)
Adverse Reactions in Pediatric Patients
Pseudotumor cerebri and slipped capital femoral epiphysis have been reported in pediatric patients receiving
levothyroxine therapy. Overtreatment may result in craniosynostosis in infants and premature closure of epiphyses in
growing pediatric patients with resultant compromised adult height.
Hypersensitivity Reactions
Hypersensitivity reactions to inactive ingredients have occurred, including: urticaria, pruritus, skin rash, flushing,
angioedema, gastrointestinal symptoms (abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea), fever, arthralgia, serum sickness,
and wheezing. Hypersensitivity to levothyroxine itself is not known to occur.

DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: DRUG INTERACTIONS | SUBSECTION: DECREASED T4- TO- T3
CONVERSION AND SPECIFIC DRUG INTERACTIONS
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Drug Interactions | Subsection: Decreased T4- to- T3 Conversion and Specific Drug
Interactions
Table 8: Drugs That May Decrease Conversion of T4 to T3
These enzyme inhibitors decrease peripheral conversion of T4 to T3, leading to decreased T3 levels. Serum T4 levels
are usually normal but may occasionally be slightly increased.
Drug or Drug Class
Effect
Beta- adrenergic antagonists (e.g.,
Propranolol >160 mg/day)
T3 and T4 levels change; TSH remains normal; patients remain clinically
euthyroid. Actions of particular beta- blockers may be impaired when a
hypothyroid patient is converted to the euthyroid state.
Glucocorticoids (e.g.,
Dexamethasone >4 mg/day)
Short- term large doses may decrease serum T3 by 30% with minimal change in
T4. Long- term therapy may result in slightly decreased T3 and T4 due to
decreased TBG production.
Amiodarone
Inhibits peripheral conversion of T4 to T3; may cause isolated biochemical
changes (increased free- T4, decreased or normal free- T3) in clinically euthyroid
patients.
7.2 – 7.8 Other Specific Drug Interactions
7.2 Antidiabetic Therapy
Addition of SYNTHROID may worsen glycemic control and increase antidiabetic agent or insulin requirements. Carefully
monitor glycemic control when thyroid therapy is started, changed, or discontinued.
7.3 Oral Anticoagulants
SYNTHROID increases the response to oral anticoagulant therapy. A decrease in the anticoagulant dose may be
warranted with correction of the hypothyroid state. Closely monitor coagulation tests.
7.4 Digitalis Glycosides
SYNTHROID may reduce the therapeutic effects of digitalis glycosides. Serum digitalis glycoside levels may decrease
when a hypothyroid patient becomes euthyroid, necessitating dose increases.
7.5 Antidepressant Therapy
Concurrent use of tricyclic (e.g., amitriptyline) or tetracyclic (e.g., maprotiline) antidepressants and SYNTHROID may
increase therapeutic and toxic effects of both drugs. Risk of cardiac arrhythmias and CNS stimulation increases.
Administration of sertraline in patients stabilized on SYNTHROID may result in increased SYNTHROID requirements.
7.6 Ketamine
Concurrent use may produce marked hypertension and tachycardia. Monitor blood pressure and heart rate closely.
7.7 Sympathomimetics
May increase the effects of sympathomimetics or thyroid hormone. Thyroid hormones may increase the risk of coronary
insufficiency when sympathomimetic agents are given to patients with coronary artery disease.
7.8 Tyrosine- Kinase Inhibitors
Concurrent use of agents such as imatinib may cause hypothyroidism. Closely monitor TSH levels.

pages.text:
DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: DRUG INTERACTIONS | SUBSECTION: DRUG- FOOD AND
DRUG- LABORATORY TEST INTERACTIONS
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Drug Interactions | Subsection: Drug- Food and Drug- Laboratory Test Interactions
7.9 Drug- Food Interactions
Consumption of certain foods may affect SYNTHROID absorption and necessitate dosage adjustments:
 Soybean flour, cottonseed meal, walnuts, and dietary fiber may bind and decrease the absorption of SYNTHROID from
the gastrointestinal tract.
 Grapefruit juice may delay the absorption of levothyroxine and reduce its bioavailability.
7.10 Drug- Laboratory Test Interactions
Thyroxine- Binding Globulin (TBG)
Consider changes in TBG concentration when interpreting T4 and T3 values. Measure and evaluate unbound (free)
hormone and/or determine the free- T4 index (FT4I) in this circumstance.
Conditions that INCREASE TBG: Pregnancy, infectious hepatitis, estrogens, estrogen- containing oral contraceptives,
acute intermittent porphyria.
Conditions that DECREASE TBG: Nephrosis, severe hypoproteinemia, severe liver disease, acromegaly, androgens,
corticosteroids.
Familial hyper- or hypo- thyroxine binding globulinemias have been described; incidence of TBG deficiency approximates
1 in 9000.
Biotin Interference
Biotin supplementation is known to interfere with thyroid hormone immunoassays that are based on a biotin- streptavidin
interaction, which may result in erroneous thyroid hormone test results. Stop biotin and biotin- containing supplements
for at least 2 days prior to thyroid testing.

DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: PREGNANCY (8.1)
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Pregnancy (8.1)
8.1 Pregnancy
Risk Summary
Clinical experience and postmarketing studies in pregnant women treated with oral levothyroxine to maintain euthyroid
state have not reported increased rates of major birth defects, miscarriages, or other adverse maternal or fetal outcomes.
There are risks to the mother and fetus associated with untreated hypothyroidism in pregnancy. SYNTHROID should
NOT be discontinued during pregnancy. Hypothyroidism diagnosed during pregnancy should be promptly treated.
Since TSH levels may increase during pregnancy, TSH should be monitored and SYNTHROID dosage adjusted during
pregnancy.
The estimated background risk of major birth defects and miscarriage in the U.S. general population in clinically
recognized pregnancies is 2% to 4% and 15% to 20%, respectively.
Clinical Considerations
Disease- Associated Maternal and/or Embryo/Fetal Risk: Maternal hypothyroidism during pregnancy is associated with a
higher rate of complications, including spontaneous abortion, gestational hypertension, pre- eclampsia, stillbirth, and
premature delivery. Untreated maternal hypothyroidism may have an adverse effect on fetal neurocognitive
development.
Dose Adjustments During Pregnancy and Postpartum: Pregnancy may increase SYNTHROID requirements. Serum TSH
levels should be monitored and dosage adjusted during pregnancy. Since postpartum TSH levels are similar to
preconception values, the SYNTHROID dosage should return to the pre- pregnancy dose immediately after delivery.

pages.text:
DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: LACTATION (8.2),
PEDIATRIC USE (8.4), GERIATRIC USE (8.5)
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Lactation (8.2), Pediatric Use (8.4), Geriatric Use
(8.5)
8.2 Lactation
Published studies report that levothyroxine is present in human milk following the administration of oral levothyroxine. No
adverse effects on the breastfed infant have been reported and there is no information on the effects of levothyroxine on
milk production.
Adequate levothyroxine treatment during lactation may normalize milk production in hypothyroid lactating mothers with
low milk supply. The developmental and health benefits of breastfeeding should be considered along with the mother's
clinical need for SYNTHROID and any potential adverse effects on the breastfed infant.
8.4 Pediatric Use
SYNTHROID is indicated in patients from birth to less than 17 years of age for replacement therapy in congenital or
acquired hypothyroidism and as an adjunct to surgery/radioiodine therapy in thyrotropin- dependent well- differentiated
thyroid cancer.
Rapid restoration of normal serum T4 concentrations is essential for preventing the adverse effects of congenital
hypothyroidism on cognitive development and overall physical growth and maturation. Initiate SYNTHROID therapy
immediately upon diagnosis. Levothyroxine is generally continued for life in these patients.
Closely monitor infants during the first 2 weeks of SYNTHROID therapy for cardiac overload and arrhythmias.
8.5 Geriatric Use
Because of the increased prevalence of cardiovascular disease among the elderly, initiate SYNTHROID at less than the
full replacement dose. Atrial fibrillation is the most common arrhythmia observed with levothyroxine overtreatment in the
elderly. Atrial arrhythmias can occur in elderly patients receiving even minimally supra- physiologic doses.

pages.text:
DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: OVERDOSAGE
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Overdosage
10. OVERDOSAGE
The signs and symptoms of overdosage are those of hyperthyroidism. In addition, confusion and disorientation may
occur. Cerebral embolism, shock, coma, and death have been reported.
Seizures occurred in a 3- year- old child ingesting 3.6 mg of levothyroxine. Symptoms may not necessarily be evident or
may not appear until several days after ingestion of levothyroxine sodium.
Signs and Symptoms of Hyperthyroid Overdosage:
Arrhythmias, myocardial infarction, dyspnea, muscle spasm, headache, nervousness, irritability, insomnia, tremors,
muscle weakness, increased appetite, weight loss, diarrhea, heat intolerance, menstrual irregularities, and skin rash.
Management
Reduce the SYNTHROID dosage or discontinue temporarily if signs or symptoms of overdosage occur. Initiate
appropriate supportive treatment as dictated by the patient's medical status.
For current information on the management of poisoning or overdosage, contact the National Poison Control Center at
1- 800- 222- 1222 or www.poison.org.

DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: DESCRIPTION
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Description
11. DESCRIPTION
SYNTHROID (levothyroxine sodium tablets, USP) is L- thyroxine (T4) and contains synthetic crystalline
L- 3,3',5,5'- tetraiodothyronine sodium salt. Synthetic T4 is chemically identical to that produced in the human thyroid
gland.
Levothyroxine (T4) sodium has the empirical formula C■■H■■I■N NaO■ · H■O, with a molecular weight of 798.86
(anhydrous).
Inactive Ingredients (all strengths):
Acacia, confectioner's sugar (contains corn starch), lactose monohydrate, magnesium stearate, povidone, and talc.
SYNTHROID tablets contain no ingredients made from a gluten- containing grain (wheat, barley, or rye).

pages.text:
DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION: MECHANISM OF
ACTION AND PHARMACODYNAMICS
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Mechanism of Action and Pharmacodynamics
12. CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY
12.1 Mechanism of Action
Thyroid hormones exert their physiologic actions through control of DNA transcription and protein synthesis.
Triiodothyronine (T3) and L- thyroxine (T4) diffuse into the cell nucleus and bind to thyroid receptor proteins attached to
DNA. This hormone- nuclear receptor complex activates gene transcription and synthesis of messenger RNA and
cytoplasmic proteins.
The physiological actions of thyroid hormones are produced predominantly by T3, the majority of which (approximately
80%) is derived from T4 by deiodination in peripheral tissues.
12.2 Pharmacodynamics
Oral levothyroxine sodium is a synthetic T4 hormone that exerts the same physiologic effect as endogenous T4, thereby
maintaining normal T4 levels when a deficiency is present.
12.3 Pharmacokinetics — Absorption and Distribution
Absorption of orally administered T4 from the gastrointestinal tract ranges from 40% to 80%. The majority of the
SYNTHROID dose is absorbed from the jejunum and upper ileum. The relative bioavailability of SYNTHROID tablets,
compared to an equal nominal dose of oral levothyroxine sodium solution, is approximately 93%.
T4 absorption is increased by fasting and decreased in malabsorption syndromes and by certain foods such as soybeans
and dietary fiber. Absorption may also decrease with age.
Distribution: Circulating thyroid hormones are greater than 99% bound to plasma proteins, including thyroxine- binding
globulin (TBG), thyroxine- binding prealbumin (TBPA), and albumin (TBA). Only unbound hormone is metabolically
active. Thyroid hormones do not readily cross the placental barrier.

pages.text:
DRUG: LEVOTHYROXINE SODIUM (SYNTHROID) | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION: PHARMACOKINETICS
— ELIMINATION AND PARAMETERS
Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Pharmacokinetics — Elimination and Parameters
12.3 Pharmacokinetics — Elimination
Metabolism
T4 is slowly eliminated. The major pathway of thyroid hormone metabolism is through sequential deiodination.
Approximately 80% of circulating T3 is derived from peripheral T4 by monodeiodination. The liver is the major site of
degradation for both T4 and T3, with T4 deiodination also occurring in the kidney and other tissues.
Approximately 80% of the daily dose of T4 is deiodinated to yield equal amounts of T3 and reverse T3 (rT3). T3 and rT3
are further deiodinated to diiodothyronine. Thyroid hormones are also metabolized via conjugation with glucuronides and
sulfates and excreted into the bile and gut where they undergo enterohepatic recirculation.
Excretion
Thyroid hormones are primarily eliminated by the kidneys. Approximately 20% of T4 is eliminated in the stool. Urinary
excretion of T4 decreases with age.

Drug: Levothyroxine Sodium (Synthroid) | Section: Nonclinical Toxicology | Subsection: Carcinogenesis, Mutagenesis, Impairment of Fertility
13. NONCLINICAL TOXICOLOGY
13.1 Carcinogenesis, Mutagenesis, Impairment of Fertility
Long- term carcinogenicity studies in animals to evaluate the carcinogenic potential of levothyroxine have not been
performed.
Studies to evaluate mutagenic potential and animal fertility have not been performed.

DRUG: METFORMIN HYDROCHLORIDE (GLUCOPHAGE) | SECTION: BOXED WARNING AND INDICATIONS AND USAGE
Drug: Metformin Hydrochloride (Glucophage) | Section: Boxed Warning and Indications and Usage
BOXED WARNING: LACTIC ACIDOSIS
Postmarketing cases of metformin- associated lactic acidosis have resulted in death, hypothermia, hypotension, and
resistant bradyarrhythmias. The onset is often subtle, accompanied only by nonspecific symptoms such as malaise,
myalgias, respiratory distress, somnolence, and abdominal pain.
Metformin- associated lactic acidosis is characterized by: elevated blood lactate concentrations (>5 mmol/L), anion gap
acidosis (without ketonuria or ketonemia), increased lactate/pyruvate ratio, and metformin plasma levels generally >5
mcg/mL.
Risk factors include: renal impairment, concomitant use of certain drugs (e.g., carbonic anhydrase inhibitors such as
topiramate), age ≥65 years, radiological study with contrast, surgery and other procedures, hypoxic states (e.g., acute
congestive heart failure), excessive alcohol intake, and hepatic impairment.
If lactic acidosis is suspected: Immediately discontinue metformin hydrochloride extended- release tablets and
institute general supportive measures in a hospital setting. Prompt hemodialysis is recommended.
1. INDICATIONS AND USAGE
Metformin hydrochloride extended- release tablets, USP are indicated as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve
glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.
Dosage Forms and Strengths: Metformin Hydrochloride Extended- Release Tablets: 500 mg and 1,000 mg for oral use.

DRUG: METFORMIN HYDROCHLORIDE (GLUCOPHAGE) | SECTION: DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION | SUBSECTION: ADULT
DOSING, RENAL IMPAIRMENT, AND CONTRAST PROCEDURES
Drug: Metformin Hydrochloride (Glucophage) | Section: Dosage and Administration | Subsection: Adult Dosing, Renal Impairment, and
Contrast Procedures
2. DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION
2.1 Adult Dosage and Administration
 Starting dose: 500 mg orally once daily with the evening meal.
 Titration: Increase in increments of 500 mg every 1 to 2 weeks based on glycemic control and tolerability, up to a
maximum of 2,000 mg once daily with the evening meal.
 Switching from immediate- release: Patients receiving metformin HCl tablets may be switched to extended- release
tablets once daily at the same total daily dose, up to 2,000 mg once daily.
 Administration: Swallow tablets whole. Never crush, cut, or chew. The inactive ingredients may occasionally appear in
feces as a soft mass — this is normal.
 Missed dose: Do not take two doses the same day. Resume at the next scheduled dose.
2.2 Recommendations for Use in Renal Impairment
eGFR (mL/min/1.73 m²)
Recommendation
≥45

30 to <45

Assess renal function with eGFR prior to initiation and at least annually thereafter. In elderly patients and those at
increased risk for renal impairment, assess renal function more frequently.
2.3 Discontinuation for Iodinated Contrast Imaging Procedures
Discontinue metformin at the time of, or prior to, iodinated contrast imaging procedures in:
 Patients with eGFR 30–60 mL/min/1.73 m²
 Patients with a history of liver disease, alcoholism, or heart failure
 Patients who will receive intra- arterial iodinated contrast
Re- evaluate eGFR 48 hours after the imaging procedure. Restart metformin if renal function is stable.

4. CONTRAINDICATIONS
Metformin hydrochloride extended- release tablets are contraindicated in patients with:
 Severe renal impairment (eGFR below 30 mL/min/1.73 m²) — due to risk of metformin accumulation and lactic
acidosis.
 Known hypersensitivity to metformin.
 Acute or chronic metabolic acidosis, including diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), with or without coma.

pages.text:
DRUG: METFORMIN HYDROCHLORIDE (GLUCOPHAGE) | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: LACTIC
ACIDOSIS — RISK FACTORS AND MANAGEMENT
Drug: Metformin Hydrochloride (Glucophage) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Lactic Acidosis — Risk Factors and
Management
5.1 Lactic Acidosis — Detailed Risk Factor Management
Metformin decreases liver uptake of lactate, increasing lactate blood levels, which may increase the risk of lactic acidosis
— especially in patients at risk. Management for each risk factor:
Risk Factor
Management Recommendation
Renal Impairment
Obtain eGFR before initiation. Contraindicated if eGFR <30. Not recommended if
eGFR 30–45. Reassess if eGFR falls below 45. Obtain eGFR at least annually;
more frequently in elderly.
Drug Interactions
Drugs impairing renal function, causing hemodynamic changes, interfering with
acid- base balance, or increasing metformin accumulation (e.g., carbonic
anhydrase inhibitors, OCT2/MATE inhibitors) increase lactic acidosis risk. Consider
more frequent monitoring.
Age ≥65 Years
Elderly patients have greater likelihood of hepatic, renal, or cardiac impairment.
Assess renal function more frequently.
Radiological Contrast Procedures
Contrast agents can acutely decrease renal function and precipitate lactic acidosis.
Discontinue metformin at time of or prior to procedure in at- risk patients.
Re- evaluate eGFR 48 hours post- procedure; restart only if renal function is stable.
Surgery and Other Procedures
Withholding food and fluids may cause volume depletion, hypotension, and renal
impairment. Temporarily discontinue metformin while patients have restricted food
and fluid intake.
Hypoxic States
Acute CHF (particularly with hypoperfusion/hypoxemia), cardiovascular collapse,
acute MI, and sepsis have been associated with lactic acidosis. Discontinue
metformin when such conditions occur.
Excessive Alcohol Intake
Alcohol potentiates metformin's effect on lactate metabolism. Warn patients against
excessive alcohol intake (including binge drinking).
Hepatic Impairment
Impaired lactate clearance results in higher lactate blood levels. Avoid use in
patients with clinical or laboratory evidence of hepatic disease.
Treatment of suspected lactic acidosis: Immediately discontinue metformin. Institute general supportive measures in
a hospital setting. Prompt hemodialysis is recommended — metformin is dialyzable with clearance up to 170 mL/min
under good hemodynamic conditions. Hemodialysis has often resulted in reversal of symptoms and recovery.

DRUG: METFORMIN HYDROCHLORIDE (GLUCOPHAGE) | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: VITAMIN
B12 DEFICIENCY, HYPOGLYCEMIA, AND MACROVASCULAR OUTCOMES
Drug: Metformin Hydrochloride (Glucophage) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Vitamin B12 Deficiency, Hypoglycemia, and
Macrovascular Outcomes
5.2 Vitamin B12 Deficiency
In clinical trials of 29- week duration with metformin HCl tablets, a decrease to subnormal levels of previously normal
serum vitamin B12 was observed in approximately 7% of patients. This decrease may be due to interference with B12
absorption from the B12- intrinsic factor complex. It may be associated with anemia but appears to be rapidly reversible
with discontinuation of metformin or vitamin B12 supplementation.
Individuals with inadequate vitamin B12 or calcium intake or absorption appear to be predisposed to developing
subnormal vitamin B12 levels.
Monitoring: Measure hematologic parameters on an annual basis and vitamin B12 at 2 to 3 year intervals in patients on
metformin. Manage any abnormalities.
5.3 Hypoglycemia with Concomitant Use with Insulin and Insulin Secretagogues
Insulin and insulin secretagogues (e.g., sulfonylureas) are known to cause hypoglycemia. Metformin may increase the
risk of hypoglycemia when combined with insulin and/or an insulin secretagogue.
Action: A lower dose of insulin or insulin secretagogue may be required to minimize the risk of hypoglycemia when
used in combination with metformin.
5.4 Macrovascular Outcomes
There have been no clinical studies establishing conclusive evidence of macrovascular risk reduction with metformin
hydrochloride extended- release tablets.

AND POSTMARKETING EXPERIENCE
Drug: Metformin Hydrochloride (Glucophage) | Section: Adverse Reactions | Subsection: Clinical Trials and Postmarketing Experience
6.1 Clinical Trials Experience
In clinical trials conducted in the U.S., over 1,000 patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus were treated with metformin
hydrochloride extended- release tablets 1,500 to 2,000 mg/day in active- controlled and placebo- controlled studies.
In the add- on to sulfonylurea study, 431 patients received metformin hydrochloride extended- release tablets + glyburide,
and 144 patients received placebo + glyburide. In 0.7% of metformin- treated patients, diarrhea was responsible for
discontinuation vs. no patients in the placebo group.

6.2 Postmarketing Experience
The following adverse reactions have been identified during post- approval use of metformin hydrochloride
extended- release tablets. Because these reactions are reported voluntarily from a population of uncertain size, it is not
always possible to reliably estimate their frequency or establish a causal relationship to drug exposure.
Hepatobiliary Disorders: Cholestatic, hepatocellular, and mixed hepatocellular liver injury have been reported with
postmarketing use of metformin.

DRUG: METFORMIN HYDROCHLORIDE (GLUCOPHAGE) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION:
PREGNANCY, LACTATION, AND REPRODUCTIVE POTENTIAL
Drug: Metformin Hydrochloride (Glucophage) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Pregnancy, Lactation, and Reproductive
Potential
8.1 Pregnancy
Risk Summary: Limited data with metformin hydrochloride extended- release tablets in pregnant women are not
sufficient to determine a drug- associated risk for major birth defects or miscarriage. Published studies with metformin use
during pregnancy have not reported a clear association with major birth defects or miscarriage risk.
There are risks to the mother and fetus associated with poorly controlled diabetes mellitus in pregnancy: maternal risks
include diabetic ketoacidosis, pre- eclampsia, spontaneous abortions, preterm delivery, stillbirth, and delivery
complications. Fetal risks include major birth defects, stillbirth, and macrosomia- related morbidity.
The estimated background risk of major birth defects is 6–10% in women with pregestational diabetes mellitus with
HbA1c >7, and up to 20–25% with HbA1c >10. In the U.S. general population, the estimated background risk of major
birth defects and miscarriage in clinically recognized pregnancies is 2–4% and 15–20%, respectively.
Animal Data: Metformin HCl was not teratogenic or embryolethal when administered to rats (doses up to 900 mg/kg
through organogenesis) or rabbits (doses up to 90 mg/kg during organogenesis). No adverse developmental effects
were observed at doses up to 3× (rat) and 1× (rabbit) a 2,000 mg clinical dose based on body surface area.
8.2 Lactation
Risk Summary: Metformin is present in human milk. Published clinical lactation studies report infant doses
approximately 0.11% to 1% of the maternal weight- adjusted dosage, and a milk/plasma ratio ranging between 0.13 and
1. There is insufficient information to determine effects on the breastfed infant or on milk production.
The developmental and health benefits of breastfeeding should be considered along with the mother's clinical need for
metformin and any potential adverse effects on the breastfed child.
8.3 Females and Males of Reproductive Potential
Discuss the potential for unintended pregnancy with premenopausal women. Therapy with metformin hydrochloride
extended- release tablets may result in ovulation in some anovulatory women, which could lead to an unintended
pregnancy.

DRUG: METFORMIN HYDROCHLORIDE (GLUCOPHAGE) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION:
PEDIATRIC, GERIATRIC, RENAL, HEPATIC USE, AND OVERDOSAGE
Drug: Metformin Hydrochloride (Glucophage) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Pediatric, Geriatric, Renal, Hepatic Use, and
Overdosage
8.4 Pediatric Use
Safety and effectiveness of metformin hydrochloride extended- release tablets in pediatric patients have not been
established.
8.5 Geriatric Use
Clinical studies did not include sufficient numbers of subjects aged 65 and over. In general, dose selection for elderly
patients should be cautious, reflecting the greater frequency of decreased hepatic, renal, or cardiac function,
concomitant disease, and other drug therapy, and the higher risk of lactic acidosis.
Assess renal function more frequently in elderly patients.
8.6 Renal Impairment
Metformin is substantially excreted by the kidney, and the risk of metformin accumulation and lactic acidosis increases
with the degree of renal impairment. Metformin hydrochloride extended- release tablets are contraindicated in patients
with eGFR below 30 mL/min/1.73 m².
8.7 Hepatic Impairment
Use of metformin in patients with hepatic impairment has been associated with cases of lactic acidosis, possibly due to
impaired lactate clearance resulting in higher lactate blood levels. Metformin hydrochloride extended- release tablets
are not recommended in patients with hepatic impairment.
10. OVERDOSAGE
Overdose of metformin HCl has occurred, including ingestion of amounts greater than 50 grams.
Complication
Incidence in Overdose Cases
Hypoglycemia
~10% of cases (no causal association established)
Lactic acidosis
~32% of overdose cases
Treatment: Metformin is dialyzable with a clearance of up to 170 mL/min under good hemodynamic conditions.
Hemodialysis may be useful for removal of accumulated drug from patients in whom metformin overdosage is suspected.

pages.text:
DRUG: METFORMIN HYDROCHLORIDE (GLUCOPHAGE) | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION: MECHANISM
OF ACTION AND PHARMACOKINETICS
Drug: Metformin Hydrochloride (Glucophage) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Mechanism of Action and Pharmacokinetics
12.1 Mechanism of Action
Metformin is a biguanide that improves glucose tolerance in patients with type 2 diabetes, lowering both basal and
postprandial plasma glucose. Metformin acts through three primary mechanisms:
 Decreases hepatic glucose production
 Decreases intestinal absorption of glucose
 Improves insulin sensitivity by increasing peripheral glucose uptake and utilization
With metformin therapy, insulin secretion remains unchanged while fasting insulin levels and day- long plasma insulin
response may decrease.
12.3 Pharmacokinetics
Absorption:
Following a single oral dose of 1,000 mg (2×500 mg tablets) after a meal, Tmax is approximately 7 to 8 hours. Once daily
1,000 mg dosing provides equivalent AUC and up to 35% higher Cmax vs. immediate- release 500 mg twice daily. At
usual clinical doses, steady state plasma concentrations are reached within 24 to 48 hours and are generally <1 mcg/mL.
Effect of food: Low- fat and high- fat meals increased AUC by approximately 38% and 73%, respectively, relative to
fasting. Both meals prolonged Tmax by approximately 3 hours but did not affect Cmax.
Distribution:
The apparent volume of distribution (V/F) following single oral doses of 850 mg averaged 654 ± 358 L. Metformin is
negligibly bound to plasma proteins. Metformin partitions into erythrocytes, most likely as a function of time.
Metabolism:
Intravenous studies demonstrate that metformin is excreted unchanged in the urine and does NOT undergo hepatic
metabolism. No metabolites have been identified in humans. No biliary excretion occurs.
Excretion:
Renal clearance is approximately 3.5× greater than creatinine clearance, indicating that tubular secretion is the major
route of elimination. Following oral administration, approximately 90% of absorbed drug is eliminated via the renal route
within the first 24 hours. Plasma elimination half- life: approximately 6.2 hours. Blood elimination half- life: approximately
17.6 hours (erythrocyte mass is a compartment of distribution).

DRUG: METFORMIN HYDROCHLORIDE (GLUCOPHAGE) | SECTION: NONCLINICAL TOXICOLOGY | SUBSECTION:
CARCINOGENESIS, MUTAGENESIS, AND IMPAIRMENT OF FERTILITY
Drug: Metformin Hydrochloride (Glucophage) | Section: Nonclinical Toxicology | Subsection: Carcinogenesis, Mutagenesis, and Impairment of
Fertility
13.1 Carcinogenesis, Mutagenesis, Impairment of Fertility
Carcinogenesis:
Long- term carcinogenicity studies were performed in Sprague Dawley rats at doses of 150, 300, and 450 mg/kg/day
(males) and 150, 450, 900, and 1,200 mg/kg/day (females) — approximately 2–8× (males) and 3–16× (females) the
maximum recommended human daily dose of 2,000 mg based on body surface area. No evidence of carcinogenicity
was found in either male or female rats.
A carcinogenicity study in Tg.AC transgenic mice at doses up to 2,000 mg applied dermally also showed no evidence of
carcinogenicity in male or female mice.
Mutagenesis:
Genotoxicity assessments were all negative, including:
 Ames test
 Gene mutation test (mouse lymphoma cells)
 Chromosomal aberrations test (human lymphocytes)
 In vivo mouse micronucleus test
Impairment of Fertility:
Fertility of male or female rats was not affected by metformin when administered at doses up to 600 mg/kg/day —
approximately 3× the maximum recommended human daily dose of 2,000 mg based on body surface area comparisons.

DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: INDICATIONS AND USAGE
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Indications and Usage
1. INDICATIONS AND USAGE
Omeprazole delayed- release capsules is a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) indicated for the following conditions:
1.1 Duodenal Ulcer (Adults)
Indicated for short- term treatment of active duodenal ulcer in adults. Most patients heal within four weeks; some may
require an additional four weeks.
Also indicated, in combination with clarithromycin and amoxicillin (triple therapy) or clarithromycin alone (dual therapy),
for treatment of patients with H. pylori infection and duodenal ulcer disease to eradicate H. pylori. Eradication reduces
the risk of duodenal ulcer recurrence.
Among patients who fail therapy, omeprazole with clarithromycin is more likely to be associated with clarithromycin
resistance vs. triple therapy. Susceptibility testing should be done in patients who fail therapy.
1.2 Gastric Ulcer (Adults)
Indicated for short- term treatment (4–8 weeks) of active benign gastric ulcer in adults.
1.3 Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) — Adults and Pediatric Patients
Symptomatic GERD: Indicated for treatment of heartburn and other symptoms associated with GERD in pediatric
patients and adults.
Erosive Esophagitis: Indicated for short- term treatment (4–8 weeks) of erosive esophagitis diagnosed by endoscopy in
pediatric patients and adults. If a patient does not respond to 8 weeks of treatment, an additional 4 weeks may be given.
If recurrence occurs, additional 4–8 week courses may be considered.
1.4 Maintenance of Healing of Erosive Esophagitis (Adults and Pediatric)
Indicated to maintain healing of erosive esophagitis in pediatric patients and adults. Controlled studies do not extend
beyond 12 months.
1.5 Pathological Hypersecretory Conditions (Adults)
Indicated for long- term treatment of pathological hypersecretory conditions (e.g., Zollinger- Ellison syndrome, multiple
endocrine adenomas, systemic mastocytosis) in adults.
Note: The safety and effectiveness in pediatric patients < 1 year of age have not been established.

2.7 Pediatric Patients (1 to 16 Years of Age)
For treatment of GERD and maintenance of healing of erosive esophagitis, recommended daily dose based on body

On a per kg basis, doses of omeprazole required to heal erosive esophagitis in pediatric patients are greater than those
for adults.
Alternative administrative options can be used for pediatric patients unable to swallow an intact capsule.
2.8 Alternative Administration Options
For patients who have difficulty swallowing capsules, the contents may be added to applesauce:
1. Add one tablespoon of applesauce to an empty bowl.
2. Open the capsule and carefully empty ALL pellets onto the applesauce.
3. Mix the pellets with the applesauce and swallow IMMEDIATELY with a glass of cool water to ensure complete
swallowing of the pellets.
Important: Applesauce should not be hot. Pellets should not be chewed or crushed. The pellets/applesauce mixture
should NOT be stored for future use.
Note: Omeprazole delayed- release capsules 40 mg were bioequivalent when administered with and without applesauce.
However, 20 mg capsules were NOT bioequivalent when administered with applesauce — a mean 25% reduction in
Cmax was observed without a significant change in AUC. Clinical relevance is unknown.

Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Dosage Forms, Strengths and Contraindications
3. DOSAGE FORMS AND STRENGTHS
Omeprazole delayed- release capsules, 40 mg: opaque, hard gelatin, apricot and amethyst colored capsules, coded 1740
on cap and 40 mg on body.
4. CONTRAINDICATIONS
Omeprazole delayed- release capsules are contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to any component of
the formulation.
Hypersensitivity reactions may include: anaphylaxis, anaphylactic shock, angioedema, bronchospasm, interstitial
nephritis, and urticaria.

pages.text:
DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: GASTRIC
MALIGNANCY, ATROPHIC GASTRITIS, AMOXICILLIN COMBINATION
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Gastric Malignancy, Atrophic Gastritis,
Amoxicillin Combination
5. WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS
5.1 Concomitant Gastric Malignancy
Symptomatic response to therapy with omeprazole does NOT preclude the presence of gastric malignancy.
5.2 Atrophic Gastritis
Atrophic gastritis has been noted occasionally in gastric corpus biopsies from patients treated long- term with
omeprazole.
5.3 Combination Use with Amoxicillin
Serious and occasionally fatal hypersensitivity (anaphylactic) reactions have been reported in patients on penicillin
therapy. These reactions are more likely in individuals with a history of penicillin hypersensitivity and/or sensitivity to
multiple allergens.
Before initiating therapy with amoxicillin, carefully inquire about previous hypersensitivity reactions to penicillins,
cephalosporins, or other allergens. If an allergic reaction occurs, discontinue amoxicillin and institute appropriate therapy.
Serious anaphylactic reactions require immediate emergency treatment with epinephrine, oxygen, intravenous steroids,
and airway management including intubation.
Pseudomembranous Colitis: Has been reported with nearly all antibacterial agents and may range from mild to
life- threatening. Consider this diagnosis in patients who present with diarrhea following antibacterial agent
administration.
Treatment with antibacterial agents alters normal colonic flora and may permit overgrowth of Clostridium difficile. A toxin
produced by C. difficile is a primary cause of antibiotic- associated colitis. In moderate to severe cases, manage with
fluids, electrolytes, protein supplementation, and treatment with an antibacterial drug effective against C. difficile.

CLARITHROMYCIN COMBINATION USE
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Clarithromycin Combination Use
5.4 Combination Use with Clarithromycin
Clarithromycin should NOT be used in pregnant women except in clinical circumstances where no alternative therapy is
appropriate. If pregnancy occurs while taking clarithromycin, the patient should be informed of the potential hazard to
the fetus.
Co- administration of omeprazole and clarithromycin has resulted in increases in plasma levels of omeprazole,
clarithromycin, and 14- hydroxy- clarithromycin.
Concomitant administration of clarithromycin with cisapride or pimozide is CONTRAINDICATED.

DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: ADVERSE REACTIONS | SUBSECTION: CLINICAL TRIALS —
MONOTHERAPY
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Adverse Reactions | Subsection: Clinical Trials — Monotherapy
6.1 Clinical Trials Experience — Omeprazole Monotherapy
Safety data reflects exposure in 3096 patients from worldwide clinical trials. The most common adverse reactions
(incidence ≥ 2%):
Adverse Reaction
Incidence
Headache
6.9%
Abdominal pain
5.2%
Nausea
4.0%
Diarrhea
3.7%
Vomiting
3.2%
Flatulence
2.7%
Additional adverse reactions with incidence ≥ 1%: acid regurgitation (1.9%), upper respiratory infection (1.9%),
constipation (1.5%), dizziness (1.5%), rash (1.5%), asthenia (1.3%), back pain (1.1%), cough (1.1%).
Pediatric Adverse Reactions
The clinical trial safety profile in pediatric patients was similar to that in adult patients. Unique to the pediatric population:
 Respiratory system events: most frequently reported in both 1 to <2 years (75.0%) and 2 to 16 years (18.5%) age
groups.
 Fever: frequently reported in the 1 to 2 year age group (33.0%).
 Accidental injuries: frequently reported in the 2 to 16 year age group (3.8%).
Combination Therapy — Dual Therapy (Omeprazole + Clarithromycin)
Adverse reactions that differed from those of omeprazole alone (n = 346): taste perversion (15%), tongue discoloration
(2%), rhinitis (2%), pharyngitis (1%), flu- syndrome (1%).
Combination Therapy — Triple Therapy (Omeprazole + Clarithromycin + Amoxicillin)
Most frequent adverse reactions (n = 274): diarrhea (14%), taste perversion (10%), headache (7%). None occurred at
higher frequency than with antimicrobial agents alone.

pages.text:
DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: DRUG INTERACTIONS
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Drug Interactions
7. DRUG INTERACTIONS
Drugs Where Gastric pH Affects Bioavailability
Because of profound and long- lasting inhibition of gastric acid secretion, omeprazole may interfere with absorption of
drugs where gastric pH is an important determinant of bioavailability (e.g., ketoconazole, ampicillin esters, and iron salts).
Drugs Metabolized by Cytochrome P450 (CYP)
Omeprazole can prolong the elimination of diazepam, warfarin, and phenytoin (metabolized by oxidation in the liver).
There have been reports of increased INR and prothrombin time in patients receiving PPIs including omeprazole and
warfarin concomitantly. Increases in INR and prothrombin time may lead to abnormal bleeding and even death.
Patients treated with omeprazole and warfarin may need to be monitored for increases in INR and prothrombin time.
Clinical reports of interaction with other CYP450- metabolized drugs: cyclosporine, disulfiram, benzodiazepines. Monitor
patients and adjust dosage if necessary.
Voriconazole
Co- administration of voriconazole (combined CYP2C19 and CYP3A4 inhibitor) with omeprazole 40 mg once daily
resulted in more than doubling of omeprazole exposure (Cmax approximately 2- fold, AUC approximately 4- fold). Dose
adjustment is not normally required. However, in patients with Zollinger- Ellison syndrome requiring higher doses (up to
240 mg/day), dose adjustment may be considered.
Atazanavir
Concomitant use of atazanavir and proton pump inhibitors is NOT RECOMMENDED. Co- administration is expected to
substantially decrease atazanavir plasma concentrations and thereby reduce its therapeutic effect.
Tacrolimus
Concomitant administration of omeprazole and tacrolimus may increase serum levels of tacrolimus.

pages.text:
DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION:
PREGNANCY (8.1)
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Pregnancy (8.1)
8.1 Pregnancy — Category C
Reproductive studies in rats and rabbits with omeprazole and multiple cohort studies in pregnant women with
omeprazole use during the first trimester do not show an increased risk of congenital anomalies or adverse pregnancy
outcomes.
There are no adequate and well- controlled studies in pregnant women. This drug should be used during pregnancy only
if clearly needed.
An expert review by TERIS (Teratogen Information System) concluded that therapeutic doses during pregnancy are
unlikely to pose a substantial teratogenic risk (data quality assessed as fair).
Key Epidemiological Studies:
Swedish Medical Birth Registry (n = 955 infants): In utero exposure to omeprazole was not associated with increased risk
of any malformation (OR 0.82, 95% CI 0.50–1.34), low birth weight, or low Apgar score. A slightly higher number of
ventricular septal defects and stillborn infants were noted, but both may be random.
Retrospective cohort study (n = 689 pregnant women; 134 exposed to omeprazole in first trimester): Overall
malformation rate 4.4%; omeprazole first- trimester malformation rate 3.6% (95% CI 1.5–8.1). Relative risk vs.
non- exposed: 0.9 (95% CI 0.3–2.2). No difference in preterm delivery or growth retardation.
Controlled prospective observational study (n = 113 women; 89% first trimester): Major congenital malformation rates:
4% (omeprazole), 2% (non- teratogen controls), 2.8% (disease controls). Spontaneous/elective abortions, preterm
deliveries, gestational age, and mean birth weight did not differ.
Animal Data
In reproductive studies in rats (up to 56x human dose) and rabbits (up to 56x human dose), no evidence of teratogenicity
was found. In pregnant rabbits at 5.5–56x human dose, dose- related increases in embryo- lethality, fetal resorptions, and
pregnancy loss occurred. In rats at 5.6–56x human dose, dose- related embryo/fetal and postnatal developmental toxicity
were observed.

pages.text:
DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: NURSING
MOTHERS (8.3), PEDIATRIC USE (8.4), GERIATRIC USE (8.5)
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Nursing Mothers (8.3), Pediatric Use (8.4),
Geriatric Use (8.5)
8.3 Nursing Mothers
Omeprazole concentrations have been measured in breast milk. Peak concentration in breast milk was less than 7% of
peak serum concentration, corresponding to 0.004 mg of omeprazole in 200 mL of milk.
Because omeprazole is excreted in human milk, because of potential for serious adverse reactions in nursing infants,
and because of the potential for tumorigenicity shown in rat carcinogenicity studies, a decision should be made whether
to discontinue nursing or to discontinue the drug, taking into account the importance of the drug to the mother.
8.4 Pediatric Use
Use in patients 1 to 16 years of age for GERD is supported by extrapolation of results from adults and
safety/pharmacokinetic studies in pediatric patients. The safety and effectiveness for GERD in patients < 1 year of age
have NOT been established. The safety and effectiveness for other pediatric uses have not been established.
8.5 Geriatric Use
Omeprazole was administered to over 2000 elderly individuals (≥ 65 years of age) in clinical trials. There were no
differences in safety and effectiveness between the elderly and younger subjects.
Pharmacokinetic differences in the elderly: The elimination rate was somewhat decreased and bioavailability was
increased. Plasma clearance averaged 250 mL/min (about half that of young volunteers) and plasma half- life averaged
one hour (about twice that of young healthy volunteers). However, NO dosage adjustment is necessary in the elderly.

DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: HEPATIC
IMPAIRMENT (8.6), RENAL IMPAIRMENT (8.7), ASIAN POPULATION (8.8)
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Hepatic Impairment (8.6), Renal
Impairment (8.7), Asian Population (8.8)
8.6 Hepatic Impairment
Consider dose reduction, particularly for maintenance of healing of erosive esophagitis.
Pharmacokinetics in hepatic impairment: Bioavailability increased to approximately 100% (reflecting decreased first- pass
effect) and plasma half- life increased to nearly 3 hours (vs. 0.5–1 hour in normals). Plasma clearance averaged 70
mL/min (vs. 500–600 mL/min in normal subjects).
8.7 Renal Impairment
No dosage reduction is necessary in patients with renal impairment.
In patients with chronic renal impairment (creatinine clearance 10–62 mL/min/1.73 m²), the disposition of omeprazole
was very similar to that in healthy volunteers, although there was a slight increase in bioavailability. Because urinary
excretion is the primary route for omeprazole metabolites, their elimination slowed in proportion to decreased creatinine
clearance.
8.8 Asian Population
Consider dose reduction, particularly for maintenance of healing of erosive esophagitis.
In pharmacokinetic studies of single 20 mg omeprazole doses, an approximately 4- fold increase in AUC was noted in
Asian subjects compared with Caucasians.

pages.title: Omeprazole.pdf
pages.text:
DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: OVERDOSAGE
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Overdosage
10. OVERDOSAGE
Reports have been received of overdosage with omeprazole in humans. Doses ranged up to 2400 mg (120 times the
usual recommended clinical dose).
Manifestations (variable): confusion, drowsiness, blurred vision, tachycardia, nausea, vomiting, diaphoresis, flushing,
headache, dry mouth, and other adverse reactions similar to those seen in normal clinical experience. Symptoms were
transient, and no serious clinical outcome has been reported when omeprazole was taken alone.
No specific antidote for omeprazole overdosage is known. Omeprazole is extensively protein bound and is therefore not
readily dialyzable.
Management: Symptomatic and supportive treatment. Consider the possibility of multiple drug ingestion. For current
information on treatment of any drug overdose, contact a Poison Control Center at 1- 800- 222- 1222.
Animal Lethal Doses:
Single oral doses of omeprazole at 1350 mg/kg (mice), 1339 mg/kg (rats), and 1200 mg/kg (dogs) were lethal. Animals
showed sedation, ptosis, tremors, convulsions, and decreased activity, body temperature, respiratory rate, and increased
depth of respiration.

DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: DESCRIPTION
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Description
11. DESCRIPTION
The active ingredient in omeprazole delayed- release capsules is a substituted benzimidazole:
5- methoxy- 2- [[(4- methoxy- 3,5- dimethyl- 2- pyridinyl)methyl]sulfinyl]- 1H- benzimidazole. It is a compound that inhibits
gastric acid secretion.
Empirical formula: C■■H■■N■O■S. Molecular weight: 345.42.
Omeprazole is a white to off- white crystalline powder that melts with decomposition at about 155°C. It is a weak base,
freely soluble in ethanol and methanol, slightly soluble in acetone and isopropanol, and very slightly soluble in water.
Stability is a function of pH — it is rapidly degraded in acid media but has acceptable stability under alkaline conditions.
Formulation
Each delayed- release capsule contains 40 mg of omeprazole in the form of enteric- coated granules.
Granule inactive ingredients: Cellulose, disodium hydrogen phosphate, hydroxypropyl cellulose, hypromellose, lactose,
mannitol, sodium lauryl sulfate.
Capsule shell inactive ingredients: Gelatin- NF, FD&C; Blue #1, FD&C; Red #40, D&C; Red #28, titanium dioxide,
synthetic black iron oxide, isopropanol, butyl alcohol, FD&C; Blue #2, D&C; Red #7 Calcium Lake. The 40 mg capsule
shells also contain D&C; Yellow #10.

DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION: MECHANISM
OF ACTION AND PHARMACODYNAMICS
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Mechanism of Action and Pharmacodynamics
12.1 Mechanism of Action
Omeprazole belongs to a class of antisecretory compounds — the substituted benzimidazoles — that suppress gastric
acid secretion by specific inhibition of the H■/K■ ATPase enzyme system at the secretory surface of the gastric parietal
cell.
Because this enzyme system is regarded as the acid (proton) pump within the gastric mucosa, omeprazole has been
characterized as a gastric acid- pump inhibitor — it blocks the final step of acid production. This effect is dose- related and
leads to inhibition of both basal and stimulated acid secretion irrespective of the stimulus.
After rapid disappearance from plasma, omeprazole can be found within the gastric mucosa for a day or more.
12.2 Pharmacodynamics — Antisecretory Activity
After oral administration, the onset of the antisecretory effect occurs within one hour, with maximum effect within two
hours. Inhibition of secretion is about 50% of maximum at 24 hours and duration of inhibition lasts up to 72 hours.
The antisecretory effect lasts far longer than would be expected from the very short (< 1 hour) plasma half- life,
apparently due to prolonged binding to the parietal H■/K■ ATPase enzyme. When the drug is discontinued, secretory
activity returns gradually over 3 to 5 days. The inhibitory effect increases with repeated once- daily dosing, reaching a
plateau after four days.

Serum Gastrin Effects
Serum gastrin levels increased during the first 1 to 2 weeks of once- daily administration. No further increase occurred
with continued treatment. Gastrin values returned to pretreatment levels usually within 1 to 2 weeks after discontinuation.

DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION:
PHARMACOKINETICS
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Pharmacokinetics
12.3 Pharmacokinetics
Absorption
Omeprazole delayed- release capsules contain an enteric- coated granule formulation (because omeprazole is
acid- labile), so absorption begins only after the granules leave the stomach. Absorption is rapid, with peak plasma levels
occurring within 0.5 to 3.5 hours.
Peak plasma concentrations (Cmax) and AUC are approximately proportional to doses up to 40 mg, but
greater- than- linear responses occur with doses > 40 mg due to saturable first- pass effect. Absolute bioavailability (vs. IV)
is about 30–40% at doses of 20–40 mg, due largely to presystemic metabolism. Plasma half- life: 0.5 to 1 hour. Total
body clearance: 500–600 mL/min.
Bioavailability increases slightly upon repeated administration.
Distribution
Protein binding is approximately 95%.
Metabolism
Omeprazole is extensively metabolized by the cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzyme system.
Excretion
Following single dose oral administration, little if any unchanged drug was excreted in urine. Approximately 77% of the
dose was eliminated in urine as at least six metabolites (including hydroxyomeprazole and the corresponding carboxylic
acid). The remainder of the dose was recoverable in feces, implying significant biliary excretion of metabolites. Three
metabolites identified in plasma (sulfide and sulfone derivatives, and hydroxyomeprazole) have very little or no
antisecretory activity.
Combination Therapy PK — Omeprazole + Clarithromycin
Steady- state plasma concentrations of omeprazole were increased when given with clarithromycin: Cmax +30%, AUC
+89%, T½ +34%. The mean 24- hour gastric pH was 5.2 with omeprazole alone and 5.7 with co- administration.
Clarithromycin concentrations were also increased: mean Cmax +10%, Cmin +27%, AUC +15%. Similar increases were
seen for 14- hydroxy- clarithromycin.

DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION:
PHARMACOKINETICS — SPECIAL POPULATIONS
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Pharmacokinetics — Special Populations
12.3 Pharmacokinetics — Special Populations
Geriatric Population
The elimination rate of omeprazole was somewhat decreased in the elderly and bioavailability was increased.
Omeprazole was 76% bioavailable in healthy elderly volunteers vs. 58% in young volunteers given the same dose.
Nearly 70% of dose was recovered in urine as metabolites. Plasma clearance: 250 mL/min (vs. 500–600 mL/min in
young subjects). Plasma half- life: approximately 1 hour (vs. 0.5 hour in young subjects).

Hepatic Impairment
In patients with chronic hepatic disease, bioavailability increased to approximately 100% (decreased first- pass effect),
plasma half- life increased to nearly 3 hours, and plasma clearance averaged 70 mL/min. Dose reduction, particularly for
maintenance of healing of erosive esophagitis, should be considered.
Renal Impairment
In patients with chronic renal impairment (Cr Cl 10–62 mL/min/1.73 m²), disposition was very similar to healthy volunteers
with slight increase in bioavailability. No dose reduction is necessary.
Asian Population
In single 20 mg dose studies, an approximately 4- fold increase in AUC was noted in Asian subjects compared with
Caucasians. Dose reduction, particularly for maintenance of healing of erosive esophagitis, should be considered.

Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Microbiology — H. pylori (12.4)
12.4 Microbiology — H. pylori
Omeprazole/clarithromycin dual therapy and omeprazole/clarithromycin/amoxicillin triple therapy have been shown to be
active against most strains of Helicobacter pylori in vitro and in clinical infections.
Pretreatment Resistance Rates:
 Clarithromycin pretreatment resistance: 3.5% in dual therapy studies; 9.3% in triple therapy studies.
 Amoxicillin pretreatment susceptible isolates (MIC ≤ 0.25 µg/mL): 99.3% of patients in triple therapy studies.

DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: NONCLINICAL TOXICOLOGY | SUBSECTION:
CARCINOGENESIS, MUTAGENESIS, IMPAIRMENT OF FERTILITY
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Nonclinical Toxicology | Subsection: Carcinogenesis, Mutagenesis, Impairment of
Fertility
13.1 Carcinogenesis, Mutagenesis, Impairment of Fertility
In two 24- month carcinogenicity studies in rats, omeprazole produced gastric ECL cell carcinoids in a dose- related
manner in both male and female rats; the incidence was markedly higher in female rats, which had higher blood levels
of omeprazole. ECL cell hyperplasia was present in all treated groups of both sexes.
Doses studied: 1.7, 3.4, 13.8, 44.0, and 140.8 mg/kg/day (approximately 0.7 to 57 times the human dose of 20 mg/day
based on body surface area). Gastric carcinoids seldom occur in untreated rats.
In a 52- week toxicity study in Sprague- Dawley rats, brain astrocytomas were found in a small number of males at 0.4, 2,
and 16 mg/kg/day. A 2- year carcinogenicity study in Sprague- Dawley rats found no astrocytomas in males or females at
the high dose of 140.8 mg/kg/day.
Mutagenicity: Omeprazole was positive for clastogenic effects in an in vitro human lymphocyte chromosomal aberration
assay, in one of two in vivo mouse micronucleus tests, and in an in vivo bone marrow cell chromosomal aberration
assay. Omeprazole was NEGATIVE in the Ames test, an in vitro mouse lymphoma cell forward mutation assay, and an
in vivo rat liver DNA damage assay.
Fertility: Omeprazole at oral doses up to 138 mg/kg/day in rats (about 56 times the human dose) was found to have no
effect on fertility and reproductive performance.
13.2 Animal Toxicology — Reproductive Toxicology
Reproductive studies in rats (up to 138 mg/kg/day) and rabbits (up to 69 mg/kg/day), approximately 56 times the human
dose, did not disclose any evidence for a teratogenic potential of omeprazole.
In rabbits, omeprazole at 5.5–56 times the human dose produced dose- related increases in embryo- lethality, fetal
resorptions, and pregnancy disruptions. In rats, dose- related embryo/fetal toxicity and postnatal developmental toxicity
were observed at 5.6–56 times the human dose.

14.5 Pathological Hypersecretory Conditions
In open studies of 136 patients with pathological hypersecretory conditions (e.g., Zollinger- Ellison syndrome, multiple
endocrine adenomas), omeprazole significantly inhibited gastric acid secretion and controlled symptoms of diarrhea,
anorexia, and pain.
Doses ranged from 20 mg every other day to 360 mg per day, maintaining basal acid secretion below 10 m Eq/hr (without
prior gastric surgery) or below 5 m Eq/hr (with prior gastric surgery). At least 11 patients with ZE syndrome on long- term
treatment developed gastric carcinoids — believed to be a manifestation of the underlying condition.

DRUG: OMEPRAZOLE DELAYED- RELEASE (PRILOSEC) | SECTION: CLINICAL STUDIES | SUBSECTION: PEDIATRIC GERD (14.6)
Drug: Omeprazole Delayed- Release (Prilosec) | Section: Clinical Studies | Subsection: Pediatric GERD (14.6)
14.6 Pediatric GERD
Symptomatic GERD in Pediatric Patients (1–16 years)
Effectiveness is based in part on data from 125 pediatric patients in two uncontrolled Phase III studies.
Study 1 (n = 12 patients, ages 1–2 years): Patients were administered a single dose of omeprazole (0.5, 1.0, or 1.5
mg/kg) for 8 weeks as an open capsule in 8.4% sodium bicarbonate solution. 75% (9/12) had vomiting/regurgitation
episodes decreased from baseline by at least 50%.
Study 2 (n = 113 patients, ages 2–16 years): Patients were administered omeprazole (10 mg or 20 mg based on body
weight) for 4 weeks. Success rates: 60% (9/15) for 10 mg and 59% (58/98) for 20 mg.
Healing of Erosive Esophagitis in Pediatric Patients (1–16 years)
In an uncontrolled, open- label dose- titration study, healing required doses ranging from 0.7 to 3.5 mg/kg/day (up to 80
mg/day). Doses were initiated at 0.7 mg/kg/day and increased in increments of 0.7 mg/kg/day if needed. 44% of patients
were healed on 0.7 mg/kg; most of the remaining patients were healed with 1.4 mg/kg after an additional 3 months.
Erosive esophagitis was healed in 51 of 57 (90%) children who completed the first course of treatment.
Maintenance of Healing of Erosive Esophagitis in Pediatric Patients
In an uncontrolled, open- label study of maintenance in 46 pediatric patients, 54% of patients required half the healing
dose. Of the 46 patients who entered the maintenance phase, 19 (41%) had no relapse. Maintenance therapy resulted in
63% of patients having no overall symptoms.
Source: Omeprazole Delayed- Release Capsules Prescribing Information. Ranbaxy Laboratories Inc. Manufactured by Merck & Co., Inc.
Revised: 8/2009. NDA019810.

Drug: Sertraline Hydrochloride (Zoloft) | Section: Boxed Warning | Subsection: Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors
BOXED WARNING: SUICIDAL THOUGHTS AND BEHAVIORS
Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior in pediatric and young adult patients in short- term
studies. Closely monitor all antidepressant- treated patients for clinical worsening and for emergence of suicidal thoughts
and behaviors.

No suicides occurred in any of the pediatric studies. Monitor all antidepressant- treated patients for clinical worsening and
emergence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors, especially during the initial few months of drug therapy and at times of
dosage changes. Counsel family members or caregivers of patients to monitor for changes in behavior. Consider
changing the therapeutic regimen, including possibly discontinuing sertraline, in patients whose depression is
persistently worse or who are experiencing emergent suicidal thoughts or behaviors.

2.1 Dosage in Patients with MDD, OCD, PD, PTSD, and SAD
A dosage of 25 mg or 50 mg per day is the initial therapeutic dosage. Subsequent dosages may be increased in case of
an inadequate response in 25 to 50 mg per day increments once a week, up to a maximum of 200 mg per day. Given the
24- hour elimination half- life, the recommended interval between dose changes is one week.

2.3 Screen for Bipolar Disorder Prior to Starting ZOLOFT
Prior to initiating treatment with sertraline or another antidepressant, screen patients for a personal or family history of
bipolar disorder, mania, or hypomania.
2.5 Switching to or from an MAOI Antidepressant
At least 14 days must elapse between discontinuation of an MAOI antidepressant and initiation of sertraline. At least 14
days must also elapse after stopping sertraline before starting an MAOI antidepressant.
2.6 Discontinuation of Treatment
Adverse reactions may occur upon discontinuation. Gradually reduce the dosage rather than stopping abruptly whenever
possible.

2.2 Dosage in Patients with PMDD
The recommended starting ZOLOFT dosage in adult women with PMDD is 50 mg per day. Sertraline may be
administered either continuously (every day throughout the menstrual cycle) or intermittently (only during the luteal
phase — starting 14 days prior to the anticipated onset of menstruation and continuing through onset of menses).

2.7 Preparation of ZOLOFT Oral Solution
The oral solution must be diluted before use. Use the supplied calibrated dropper (with 25 mg and 50 mg graduation
marks) to measure the required amount. Mix with 4 ounces (1/2 cup) of water, ginger ale, lemon/lime soda, lemonade, or
orange juice ONLY. A slight haze may appear after mixing — this is normal. Instruct patients to take the dose
immediately after mixing.

DRUG: SERTRALINE HYDROCHLORIDE (ZOLOFT) | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: SUICIDALITY
AND SEROTONIN SYNDROME
Drug: Sertraline Hydrochloride (Zoloft) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Suicidality and Serotonin Syndrome
5.1 Suicidal Thoughts and Behaviors in Pediatric and Young Adult Patients
In pooled analyses of placebo- controlled trials including approximately 77,000 adult patients and over 4,400 pediatric
patients, the incidence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors in pediatric and young adult patients was greater in
antidepressant- treated patients than in placebo- treated patients. It is unknown whether the risk extends to longer- term
use beyond four months.
5.2 Serotonin Syndrome
SSRIs including sertraline can precipitate serotonin syndrome, a potentially life- threatening condition. Risk is increased
with concomitant use of other serotonergic drugs (triptans, TCAs, fentanyl, lithium, tramadol, tryptophan, buspirone,
amphetamines, St. John's Wort) and with MAOIs. Serotonin syndrome can also occur when these drugs are used alone.
Signs and Symptoms of Serotonin Syndrome:
Category
Signs and Symptoms
Mental status
Agitation, hallucinations, delirium, coma
Autonomic instability
Tachycardia, labile blood pressure, dizziness, diaphoresis, flushing, hyperthermia
Neuromuscular
Tremor, rigidity, myoclonus, hyperreflexia, incoordination
Gastrointestinal
Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea
Other
Seizures
Discontinue sertraline and any concomitant serotonergic agents immediately if serotonin syndrome symptoms occur, and
initiate supportive symptomatic treatment. Do not initiate sertraline in a patient being treated with MAOIs such as linezolid
or IV methylene blue.

pages.text:
DRUG: SERTRALINE HYDROCHLORIDE (ZOLOFT) | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: BLEEDING,
MANIA, DISCONTINUATION, SEIZURES
Drug: Sertraline Hydrochloride (Zoloft) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Bleeding, Mania, Discontinuation, Seizures
5.3 Increased Risk of Bleeding
Drugs that interfere with serotonin reuptake, including sertraline, increase the risk of bleeding. Concomitant use of
aspirin, NSAIDs, other antiplatelet drugs, warfarin, and other anticoagulants may add to this risk. Bleeding events have
ranged from ecchymosis, hematoma, epistaxis, and petechiae to life- threatening hemorrhages. For patients taking
warfarin, carefully monitor the international normalized ratio (INR).
5.4 Activation of Mania or Hypomania
In patients with bipolar disorder, treating a depressive episode with sertraline may precipitate a mixed/manic episode.
Symptoms of mania or hypomania were reported in 0.4% of patients treated with ZOLOFT in controlled clinical trials.
Screen patients for any personal or family history of bipolar disorder, mania, or hypomania prior to initiating treatment.
5.5 Discontinuation Syndrome
Adverse reactions after discontinuation include: nausea, sweating, dysphoric mood, irritability, agitation, dizziness,
sensory disturbances (e.g., paresthesia, electric shock sensations), tremor, anxiety, confusion, headache, lethargy,
emotional lability, insomnia, hypomania, tinnitus, and seizures. A gradual reduction in dosage rather than abrupt
cessation is recommended whenever possible.
5.6 Seizures
ZOLOFT has not been systematically evaluated in patients with seizure disorders. Patients with a history of seizures
were excluded from clinical studies. Prescribe with caution in patients with a seizure disorder.

DRUG: SERTRALINE HYDROCHLORIDE (ZOLOFT) | SECTION: WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS | SUBSECTION: GLAUCOMA,
HYPONATREMIA, QTC, SEXUAL DYSFUNCTION
Drug: Sertraline Hydrochloride (Zoloft) | Section: Warnings and Precautions | Subsection: Glaucoma, Hyponatremia, QTc, Sexual Dysfunction
5.7 Angle- Closure Glaucoma
The pupillary dilation that occurs following use of many antidepressants including sertraline may trigger an angle closure
attack in patients with anatomically narrow angles who do not have a patent iridectomy. Avoid use of antidepressants
including sertraline in patients with untreated anatomically narrow angles.
5.8 Hyponatremia
Hyponatremia may occur with SSRIs including sertraline. Cases with serum sodium lower than 110 mmol/L have been
reported. Signs and symptoms include headache, difficulty concentrating, memory impairment, confusion, weakness,
and unsteadiness. Severe cases: hallucination, syncope, seizure, coma, respiratory arrest, death. This appears related
to SIADH in many cases. Elderly patients, patients taking diuretics, and those who are volume- depleted are at greater
risk. Discontinue sertraline in patients with symptomatic hyponatremia.
5.10 QTc Prolongation
Cases of QTc prolongation and Torsade de Pointes (TdP) have been reported during post- marketing use. In a thorough
QTc study in 54 healthy adult subjects, there was a positive relationship between QTc interval length and serum
sertraline concentration. Use with caution in patients with risk factors for QTc prolongation.
5.11 Sexual Dysfunction
Use of SSRIs including sertraline may cause symptoms of sexual dysfunction. In males: ejaculatory delay or failure,
decreased libido, erectile dysfunction. In females: decreased libido and delayed or absent orgasm. Inquire about sexual
function prior to initiation and specifically about changes during treatment, as sexual function may not be spontaneously
reported.

OCD, PD, PTSD, SAD, and PMDD:

Adverse Reactions in Pediatric Patients
In 281 pediatric patients treated with sertraline in placebo- controlled studies, the overall adverse reaction profile was
generally similar to adults. Additional adverse reactions reported in at least 2% of pediatric patients at twice the placebo
rate (not appearing in adult table): fever, hyperkinesia, urinary incontinence, aggression, epistaxis, purpura, arthralgia,
decreased weight, muscle twitching, and anxiety.
6.2 Post- marketing Experience
Serious post- marketing adverse reactions include: QTc prolongation and Torsade de Pointes, severe liver events
(hepatitis, jaundice, liver failure with some fatal outcomes), serotonin syndrome, extrapyramidal symptoms (akathisia,
dystonia), hyponatremia, Stevens- Johnson Syndrome (SJS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), pulmonary
hypertension, agranulocytosis, aplastic anemia, rhabdomyolysis, acute renal failure, cerebrovascular spasm.
