DRUG: SERTRALINE HYDROCHLORIDE (ZOLOFT) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: PREGNANCY
Drug: Sertraline Hydrochloride (Zoloft) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Pregnancy
8.1 Pregnancy
Available published epidemiologic studies of pregnant women exposed to sertraline in the first trimester suggest no
difference in major birth defect risk compared to the background rate. A meta- analysis suggests no increase in total
malformations (OR=1.01, 95% CI 0.88–1.17) or cardiac malformations (OR=0.93, 95% CI 0.70–1.23).
Third Trimester Exposure — Neonatal Risks:
Exposure to SSRIs including sertraline in late pregnancy may lead to increased risk of neonatal complications requiring
prolonged hospitalization, respiratory support, and tube feeding. Reported neonatal clinical findings: respiratory distress,
cyanosis, apnea, seizures, temperature instability, feeding difficulty, hypoglycemia, hypotonia, hypertonia, hyperreflexia,
tremor, jitteriness, irritability, constant crying. These may represent direct toxic effects or drug discontinuation syndrome.
Persistent Pulmonary Hypertension of the Newborn (PPHN):
Exposure to SSRIs after the 20th week of gestation was associated with approximately 6- fold higher risk of PPHN
compared to unexposed infants in one retrospective case- control study. PPHN occurs in 1–2 per 1,000 live births and is
associated with substantial neonatal morbidity and mortality. Monitor neonates exposed to sertraline in the third
trimester for PPHN and drug discontinuation syndrome.
ZOLOFT oral solution contains 12% alcohol and is not recommended during pregnancy because there is no known safe
level of alcohol exposure during pregnancy.

DRUG: SERTRALINE HYDROCHLORIDE (ZOLOFT) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: LACTATION AND
PEDIATRIC USE
Drug: Sertraline Hydrochloride (Zoloft) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Lactation and Pediatric Use
8.2 Lactation
Available data from published literature demonstrate low levels of sertraline and its metabolites in human milk. In a
pooled analysis of 53 mother- infant pairs, exclusively human milk- fed infants had an average of 2% (range 0%–15%) of
the sertraline serum levels measured in their mothers. No adverse reactions were observed in these infants. The
developmental and health benefits of breastfeeding should be considered along with the mother's clinical need for
sertraline.
8.4 Pediatric Use
Safety and efficacy of sertraline have been established for OCD in pediatric patients aged 6 to 17. Safety and
effectiveness have NOT been established for indications other than OCD in pediatric patients. Monitor all pediatric
patients treated with antidepressants for clinical worsening, suicidal thoughts, and unusual changes in behavior,
especially during the initial few months of treatment.
Weight and Growth Monitoring in Pediatric Patients:
In pooled 10- week MDD trials (n=373), there was a weight difference of approximately 1 kg between sertraline and
placebo groups, representing slight weight loss for sertraline vs. slight gain for placebo, in both children (ages 6–11) and
adolescents (ages 12–17). Monitor weight and growth in pediatric patients treated with sertraline.
Juvenile Animal Data:
A study in juvenile rats at clinically relevant doses showed delay in sexual maturation but no effect on fertility in males or
females. No effect on neurobehavioral development was observed up to the highest dose tested (80 mg/kg/day).

pages.text:
DRUG: SERTRALINE HYDROCHLORIDE (ZOLOFT) | SECTION: USE IN SPECIFIC POPULATIONS | SUBSECTION: GERIATRIC,
HEPATIC, AND RENAL IMPAIRMENT
Drug: Sertraline Hydrochloride (Zoloft) | Section: Use in Specific Populations | Subsection: Geriatric, Hepatic, and Renal Impairment
8.5 Geriatric Use
Of the total ZOLOFT clinical study population, 17% were ≥65 years old and 4% were ≥75 years old. No overall
differences in safety or effectiveness were observed between elderly and younger subjects. In general, dose selection for
elderly patients should be conservative, usually starting at the low end of the dosing range, reflecting greater frequency
of decreased hepatic, renal, or cardiac function. Sertraline plasma clearance is approximately 40% lower in elderly
patients vs. younger individuals. SSRIs including sertraline have been associated with clinically significant hyponatremia
in elderly patients.
8.6 Hepatic Impairment
In patients with mild hepatic impairment, sertraline clearance was reduced, resulting in approximately 3- fold greater
exposure compared to volunteers with normal hepatic function. Use half the recommended dosage in patients with mild
hepatic impairment (Child- Pugh score 5 or 6). Not recommended in moderate (score 7–10) or severe (score 10–15)
impairment because sertraline is extensively metabolized and the effects have not been studied in these populations.
8.7 Renal Impairment
No dose adjustment is needed in patients with mild to severe renal impairment. Sertraline is extensively metabolized and
excretion of unchanged drug in urine is a minor route of elimination. In volunteers with mild to moderate (CLcr 30–60
mL/min), moderate to severe (CLcr 10–29 mL/min), and severe (receiving hemodialysis) renal impairment (N=10 per
group), pharmacokinetics and protein binding were not altered compared to age- matched volunteers with no renal
impairment.

DRUG: SERTRALINE HYDROCHLORIDE (ZOLOFT) | SECTION: OVERDOSAGE
Drug: Sertraline Hydrochloride (Zoloft) | Section: Overdosage
10. OVERDOSAGE
The following have been reported with sertraline tablet overdosage:
Overdosage Effect
Details
Seizures and altered mental status
Seizures may be delayed; altered mental status including coma
Cardiovascular toxicity
May be delayed; includes QRS and QTc interval prolongation. Hypertension
most commonly seen; rarely hypotension alone or with co- ingestants including
alcohol
Serotonin syndrome
Patients with multiple drug overdosage involving other proserotonergic drugs
may have higher risk
Management:
Gastrointestinal decontamination with activated charcoal should be considered in patients who present early after a
sertraline overdose. Contact a Poison Control Center (1- 800- 221- 2222) or a medical toxicologist for additional
overdosage management recommendations.
9. Drug Abuse and Dependence
ZOLOFT contains sertraline, which is NOT a controlled substance. In a placebo- controlled, double- blind, randomized
study comparing the abuse liability of sertraline, alprazolam, and d- amphetamine, sertraline did not produce the positive
subjective effects indicative of abuse potential (e.g., euphoria or drug liking) that were observed with the other two drugs.

pages.text:
DRUG: SERTRALINE HYDROCHLORIDE (ZOLOFT) | SECTION: CLINICAL PHARMACOLOGY | SUBSECTION: MECHANISM OF
ACTION AND PHARMACODYNAMICS
Drug: Sertraline Hydrochloride (Zoloft) | Section: Clinical Pharmacology | Subsection: Mechanism of Action and Pharmacodynamics
12.1 Mechanism of Action
Sertraline potentiates serotonergic activity in the central nervous system through inhibition of neuronal reuptake of
serotonin (5- HT). Sertraline is a potent and selective inhibitor of neuronal serotonin reuptake and has only very weak
effects on norepinephrine and dopamine neuronal reuptake. In vitro studies show sertraline has no significant affinity for
adrenergic, cholinergic, GABA, dopaminergic, histaminergic, serotonergic (5HT1A, 5HT1B, 5HT2), or benzodiazepine
receptors. Sertraline does not inhibit monoamine oxidase.
12.2 Pharmacodynamics — Cardiac Electrophysiology
In a randomized, double- blind, placebo- and positive- controlled thorough QTc study in 54 healthy adult subjects at 2- fold
the maximum recommended daily dose (~3- fold the steady- state exposure), the largest mean ΔΔQTc was 10 ms with
upper bound of two- sided 90% CI of 12 ms. The QTc interval length was positively correlated with serum concentrations
of sertraline and N- desmethylsertraline. Sertraline blocks the uptake of serotonin into human platelets at clinically
relevant doses.
In healthy subjects, the acute cognitive and psychomotor effects of alcohol were not potentiated by sertraline.

DRUG: SERTRALINE HYDROCHLORIDE (ZOLOFT) | SECTION: CLINICAL STUDIES | SUBSECTION: MAJOR DEPRESSIVE
DISORDER AND OCD
Drug: Sertraline Hydrochloride (Zoloft) | Section: Clinical Studies | Subsection: Major Depressive Disorder and OCD
14.1 Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)
Efficacy was established in two randomized, double- blind, placebo- controlled studies (MDD- 1: 8- week, 3- arm
flexible- dose study; MDD- 2: 6- week multicenter parallel fixed- dose study at 50, 100, and 200 mg/day) and one
randomized- withdrawal maintenance study (MDD- 3). ZOLOFT was superior to placebo on the Hamilton Rating Scale for
Depression (HAMD- 17) and Clinical Global Impression (CGI- S and CGI- I) scores.
In the maintenance study (MDD- 3), patients (n=295) who responded during an 8- week open treatment phase on
sertraline 50–200 mg/day were randomized to continuation on sertraline or placebo for 44 weeks. Relapse rate:
sertraline 8% (n=11/136) vs. placebo 39% (n=31/80). Mean sertraline dose for completers: 70 mg/day.
14.2 Obsessive- Compulsive Disorder (OCD)
Efficacy established in three multicenter placebo- controlled adult studies and one pediatric study (ages 6–17). Studies
used the Yale- Brown Obsessive- Compulsive Scale (Y- BOCS) as the primary outcome measure.

DRUG: SERTRALINE HYDROCHLORIDE (ZOLOFT) | SECTION: CLINICAL STUDIES | SUBSECTION: PANIC DISORDER, PTSD,
SAD, AND PMDD
Drug: Sertraline Hydrochloride (Zoloft) | Section: Clinical Studies | Subsection: Panic Disorder, PTSD, SAD, and PMDD
14.3 Panic Disorder (PD)
Effectiveness demonstrated in three double- blind, placebo- controlled studies. Studies PD- 1 and PD- 2 were 10- week
flexible- dose studies (sertraline initiated at 25 mg/day, titrated to 200 mg/day max). ZOLOFT was statistically significantly
more effective than placebo on panic attack frequency reduction (difference of ~2 fewer panic attacks/week) and CGI
scores. Study PD- 3 was a 12- week fixed- dose study at 50, 100, and 200 mg/day confirming efficacy. Maintenance study
(PD- 4): significantly lower relapse rates over 28 weeks vs. placebo.
14.4 Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
Effectiveness established in two 12- week multicenter placebo- controlled studies (PSTD- 1 and PSTD- 2) with flexible
dosing (25 mg/day titrated to 200 mg/day max). Mean duration of PTSD for enrolled patients: 12 years; 44% had
secondary depressive disorder. ZOLOFT showed statistically significant improvement vs. placebo on the CAPS, IES,
and CGI scores. Post hoc analyses showed significant effects in women but essentially no effect in the smaller number
of men. Maintenance study (PSTD- 5): significantly lower relapse rates over 28 weeks vs. placebo.
14.5 Social Anxiety Disorder (SAD)
Effectiveness established in two multicenter randomized placebo- controlled studies (SAD- 1: 12- week, SAD- 2: 20- week
flexible- dose). ZOLOFT was statistically significantly more effective than placebo on the Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale
(LSAS), Duke Brief Social Phobia Scale (BSPS), Marks Fear Questionnaire Social Phobia Subscale, and CGI- I
responder rates. Maintenance study (SAD- 3): significantly lower relapse rate vs. placebo.
14.6 Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder (PMDD)
Effectiveness established in two double- blind, placebo- controlled flexible- dose trials over 3 menstrual cycles. PMDD- 1
(N=251) used continuous daily dosing (50–150 mg/day, mean 102 mg/day). PMDD- 2 (N=281) used intermittent
luteal- phase dosing (50–100 mg/day during luteal phase only, mean 74 mg/day). Both studies showed statistically
significant superiority over placebo on the Daily Record of Severity of Problems (DRSP), HAMD- 17, and CGI scores.

DRUG: SERTRALINE HYDROCHLORIDE (ZOLOFT) | SECTION: NONCLINICAL TOXICOLOGY | SUBSECTION: CARCINOGENESIS,
MUTAGENESIS, FERTILITY
Drug: Sertraline Hydrochloride (Zoloft) | Section: Nonclinical Toxicology | Subsection: Carcinogenesis, Mutagenesis, Fertility
13.1 Carcinogenesis, Mutagenesis, Impairment of Fertility
Carcinogenesis:
Lifetime carcinogenicity studies were carried out in CD- 1 mice and Long- Evans rats at doses up to 40 mg/kg/day (1x and
2x MRHD of 200 mg/day on a mg/m² basis). Findings: dose- related increase of liver adenomas in male mice (10–40
mg/kg); increase in follicular adenomas of the thyroid in female rats at 40 mg/kg. No increase in hepatocellular
carcinomas. Liver adenomas have a variable rate of spontaneous occurrence in CD- 1 mice and are of unknown
significance to humans.
Mutagenesis:
Sertraline had no genotoxic effects with or without metabolic activation based on: bacterial mutation assay, mouse
lymphoma mutation assay, and tests for cytogenetic aberrations in vivo in mouse bone marrow and in vitro in human
lymphocytes.
Impairment of Fertility:
A decrease in fertility was seen in one of two rat studies at a dose of 80 mg/kg (3.1 times the MRHD on a mg/m² basis in
adolescents).
